text:
# LCD Control

## FF40 — LCDC: LCD control

**LCDC** is the main **LCD C**ontrol register at address $FF40. Its bits toggle what elements are displayed on the screen, and how.

Bit layout:
- Bit 7: LCD & PPU enable (0 = Off; 1 = On)
- Bit 6: Window tile map area (0 = $9800–$9BFF; 1 = $9C00–$9FFF)
- Bit 5: Window enable (0 = Off; 1 = On)
- Bit 4: BG & Window tile data area (0 = $8800–$97FF; 1 = $8000–$8FFF)
- Bit 3: BG tile map area (0 = $9800–$9BFF; 1 = $9C00–$9FFF)
- Bit 2: OBJ size (0 = 8×8; 1 = 8×16)
- Bit 1: OBJ enable (0 = Off; 1 = On)
- Bit 0: BG & Window enable / priority (Different meaning in CGB Mode)

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control

text:
### LCDC.7 — LCD enable

This bit controls whether the LCD is on and the PPU is active. Setting it to 0 turns both off, which grants immediate and full access to VRAM, OAM, etc.

**CAUTION**: Stopping LCD operation (Bit 7 from 1 to 0) may be performed during VBlank ONLY. Disabling the display outside of the VBlank period may damage the hardware by burning in a black horizontal line similar to that which appears when the GB is turned off. This appears to be a serious issue. Nintendo is reported to reject any games not following this rule.

When the display is disabled the screen is blank, which on DMG is displayed as a white whiter than color #0.

On SGB, the screen doesn't turn white; it appears that the previous picture sticks to the screen.

When re-enabling the LCD, the PPU will immediately start drawing again, but the screen will stay blank during the first frame.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.7 — LCD enable

text:
### LCDC.6 — Window tile map area

This bit controls which background map the Window uses for rendering. When it's clear (0), the $9800 tilemap is used, otherwise it's the $9C00 one.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.6 — Window tile map area

text:
### LCDC.5 — Window enable

This bit controls whether the window shall be displayed or not.

This bit is overridden on DMG by [bit 0](#LCDC.0) if that bit is clear.

Changing the value of this register mid-frame triggers several more complex behaviours; see the Window mid-frame behavior chapter for details.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.5 — Window enable

text:
### LCDC.4 — BG and Window tile data area

This bit controls which addressing mode the BG and Window use to pick tiles.

- Bit 4 = 0: Tile data area is $8800–$97FF (signed tile IDs)
- Bit 4 = 1: Tile data area is $8000–$8FFF (unsigned tile IDs)

Objects (sprites) aren't affected by this, and will always use the $8000 addressing mode.

metadata.section: LCDC.4 — BG and Window tile data area
metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.4 — BG and Window tile data area

text:
### LCDC.3 — BG tile map area

This bit works similarly to LCDC bit 6: if the bit is clear (0), the BG uses tilemap $9800, otherwise tilemap $9C00.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.3 — BG tile map area

text:
### LCDC.2 — OBJ size

This bit controls the size of all objects (1 tile or 2 stacked vertically).

- Bit 2 = 0: Objects are 8×8 pixels
- Bit 2 = 1: Objects are 8×16 pixels

Be cautious when changing object size mid-frame. Changing from 8×8 to 8×16 pixels mid-frame within 8 scanlines of the bottom of an object causes the object's second tile to be visible for the rest of those 8 lines.

If the size is changed during mode 2 or 3, remnants of objects in range could leak into the other tile and cause artifacts.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.2 — OBJ size

text:
### LCDC.1 — OBJ enable

This bit toggles whether objects are displayed or not.

This can be toggled mid-frame, for example to avoid objects being displayed on top of a status bar or text box.

Note: Toggling mid-scanline might have funky results on DMG; investigation needed.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.1 — OBJ enable

text:
### LCDC.0 — BG and Window enable/priority

LCDC.0 has different meanings depending on Game Boy type and Mode:

**Non-CGB Mode (DMG, SGB and CGB in compatibility mode): BG and Window display**
When Bit 0 is cleared, both background and window become blank (white), and the Window Display Bit (bit 5) is ignored in that case. Only objects may still be displayed (if enabled in Bit 1).

**CGB Mode: BG and Window master priority**
When Bit 0 is cleared, the background and window lose their priority — the objects will always be displayed on top of background and window, independently of the priority flags in OAM and BG Map attributes.

When Bit 0 is set, pixel priority is resolved as described in the BG-to-OBJ Priority in CGB Mode section.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.0 — BG and Window enable/priority

text:
## Using LCDC

LCDC is a powerful tool: each bit controls a lot of behavior, and can be modified at any time during the frame.

One of the important aspects of LCDC is that unlike VRAM, the PPU never locks it. It's thus possible to modify it mid-scanline!

## Faux-layer textbox/status bar

A problem often seen in 8-bit games is objects rendering on top of the textbox/status bar. It's possible to prevent this using LCDC if the textbox/status bar is alone on its scanlines:

- Set LCDC.1 to 1 for gameplay scanlines
- Set LCDC.1 to 0 for textbox/status bar scanlines

Usually, these bars are either at the top or bottom of the screen, so the bit can be set by the VBlank and/or STAT handlers.

Hiding objects behind a right-side window is more challenging.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > Using LCDC

text:
# LCD Status Registers

**Terminology**: A *dot* is the shortest period over which the PPU can output one pixel. It is equivalent to 1 T-cycle on DMG or on CGB Normal Speed mode, or 2 T-cycles on CGB Double Speed mode. On each dot during mode 3, either the PPU outputs a pixel or the fetcher is stalling the FIFOs.

## FF44 — LY: LCD Y coordinate [read-only]

LY indicates the current horizontal line, which might be about to be drawn, being drawn, or just been drawn. LY can hold any value from 0 to 153, with values from 144 to 153 indicating the VBlank period.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Status Registers > FF44 — LY: LCD Y coordinate [read-only]

text:
## FF45 — LYC: LY compare

The Game Boy constantly compares the value of the LYC and LY registers. When both values are identical, the LYC=LY flag in the STAT register is set, and (if enabled) a STAT interrupt is requested.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Status Registers > FF45 — LYC: LY compare

text:
## FF41 — STAT: LCD status

Bit layout:
- Bit 7: (unused)
- Bit 6: LYC int select (Read/Write) — If set, selects the LYC == LY condition for the STAT interrupt.
- Bit 5: Mode 2 int select (Read/Write) — If set, selects the Mode 2 condition for the STAT interrupt.
- Bit 4: Mode 1 int select (Read/Write) — If set, selects the Mode 1 condition for the STAT interrupt.
- Bit 3: Mode 0 int select (Read/Write) — If set, selects the Mode 0 condition for the STAT interrupt.
- Bit 2: LYC == LY (Read-only) — Set when LY contains the same value as LYC; it is constantly updated.
- Bits 1-0: PPU mode (Read-only) — Indicates the PPU's current status. Reports 0 instead when the PPU is disabled.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Status Registers > FF41 — STAT: LCD status

text:
### Spurious STAT interrupts

A hardware quirk in the monochrome Game Boy makes the LCD interrupt sometimes trigger when writing to STAT (including writing $00) during OAM scan, HBlank, VBlank, or LY=LYC. It behaves as if $FF were written for one M-cycle, and then the written value were written the next M-cycle.

Because the GBC in DMG mode does not have this quirk, two games that depend on this quirk (Ocean's Road Rash and Vic Tokai's Xerd no Densetsu) will not run on a GBC.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Status Registers > FF41 — STAT: LCD status > Spurious STAT interrupts

text:
## FF47 — BGP (Non-CGB Mode only): BG palette data

This register assigns gray shades to the color indices of the BG and Window tiles.

Bit layout:
- Bits 7-6: Color for ID 3
- Bits 5-4: Color for ID 2
- Bits 3-2: Color for ID 1
- Bits 1-0: Color for ID 0

Each two-bit value maps to a color:
- Value 0: White
- Value 1: Light gray
- Value 2: Dark gray
- Value 3: Black

In CGB Mode the color palettes are taken from CGB palette memory (CRAM) instead.

metadata.section: FF47 — BGP (Non-CGB Mode only): BG palette data
metadata.heading_path: Palettes > LCD Monochrome Palettes > FF47 — BGP

text:
## FF48–FF49 — OBP0, OBP1 (Non-CGB Mode only): OBJ palette 0, 1 data

These registers assign gray shades to the color indexes of the OBJs that use the corresponding palette.
They work exactly like BGP ($FF47), except that the lower two bits are ignored because color index 0 is transparent for OBJs.

metadata.heading_path: Palettes > LCD Monochrome Palettes > FF48–FF49 — OBP0, OBP1

text:
## LCD Color Palettes (CGB only)

The CGB has a small amount of RAM used to store its color palettes. Palette RAM (or CRAM for Color RAM) is not accessed directly, but instead through registers.

### FF68 — BCPS/BGPI (CGB Mode only): Background color palette specification / Background palette index

This register addresses a byte in the CGB's background palette RAM. There are 8 palettes × 4 colors/palette × 2 bytes/color = 64 bytes that can be addressed.

Order: BGP0 color 0, BGP0 color 1, BGP0 color 2, BGP0 color 3, BGP1 color 0, and so on. Address $03 accesses the second (upper) byte of BGP0 color #1 via BCPD.

Bit layout:
- Bit 7: Auto-increment (0 = Disabled; 1 = Increment Address field after writing to BCPD/OCPD, even during Mode 3 despite the write failing; reads never cause increment)
- Bits 5-0: Address — Specifies which byte of BG Palette Memory can be accessed

Unlike BCPD, this register can be accessed outside VBlank and HBlank.

### FF69 — BCPD/BGPD (CGB Mode only): Background color palette data / Background palette data

This register reads/writes data to the CGB background palette memory, addressed through BCPS. Each color is stored as little-endian RGB555:
- Bits 0-4: Red intensity
- Bits 5-9: Green intensity
- Bits 10-14: Blue intensity

Much like VRAM, data in palette memory cannot be read or written during Mode 3 (when the PPU is reading from it).

All background colors are initialized as white by the boot ROM, but it is a good idea to initialize all colors yourself (e.g., for soft-reset).

metadata.heading_path: Palettes > LCD Color Palettes (CGB only) > BCPS/BCPD

text:
### FF6A–FF6B — OCPS/OBPI, OCPD/OBPD (CGB Mode only): OBJ color palette specification / OBJ palette index, OBJ color palette data / OBJ palette data

These registers function exactly like BCPS and BCPD respectively; the 64 bytes of OBJ palette memory are entirely separate from Background palette memory, but function the same.

Note that while 4 colors are stored per OBJ palette, color #0 is never used, as it's always transparent. It's fine to write garbage values or leave color #0 uninitialized.

In CGB mode, the boot ROM leaves all object colors uninitialized (and thus somewhat random/unreliable), aside from setting the first byte of OBJ0 color #0 to $00, which is unused.

In DMG compatibility mode, the boot ROM sets the first 2 object palettes which are used by OBP0/OBP1.

metadata.heading_path: Palettes > LCD Color Palettes (CGB only) > OCPS/OCPD

text:
### RGB Translation by CGBs

When developing graphics on PCs, note that the RGB values will have different appearance on CGB displays as on VGA/HDMI monitors calibrated to sRGB color. Because the GBC is not lit, the highest intensity will produce light gray rather than white. The intensities are not linear; the values $10-$1F will all appear very bright, while medium and darker colors are ranged at $00-0F.

The CGB display's pigments aren't perfectly saturated. Increasing the intensity of only one R/G/B color will also influence the other two R/G/B colors. For example, a color setting of $03EF (Blue=$00, Green=$1F, Red=$0F) will appear as Neon Green on VGA displays, but on the CGB it'll produce a decently washed out Yellow.

metadata.heading_path: Palettes > LCD Color Palettes (CGB only) > RGB Translation by CGBs

text:
### RGB Translation by GBAs

Even though GBA is described to be compatible with CGB games, most CGB games are completely unplayable on older GBAs because most colors are invisible (black). Colors such as Black and White appear the same on both CGB and GBA, but medium intensities are arranged completely different. Intensities in range $00–07 are invisible/black on older GBAs.

**Workaround**: Newer CGB games may avoid this effect by changing palette data when detecting GBA hardware. Based on measurements using the 144p Test Suite, a fairly close approximation is: GBA = GBC × 3/4 + $08 for each R/G/B component.

This problem with low brightness levels does not affect later GBA SP units and Game Boy Player.

metadata.heading_path: Palettes > LCD Color Palettes (CGB only) > RGB Translation by GBAs

text:
# Memory Map

The Game Boy has a 16-bit address bus, which is used to address ROM, RAM, and I/O.

| Start | End | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0000 | $3FFF | 16 KiB ROM bank 00 | From cartridge, usually a fixed bank |
| $4000 | $7FFF | 16 KiB ROM Bank 01–NN | From cartridge, switchable bank via mapper (if any) |
| $8000 | $9FFF | 8 KiB Video RAM (VRAM) | In CGB mode, switchable bank 0/1 |
| $A000 | $BFFF | 8 KiB External RAM | From cartridge, switchable bank if any |
| $C000 | $CFFF | 4 KiB Work RAM (WRAM) | |
| $D000 | $DFFF | 4 KiB Work RAM (WRAM) | In CGB mode, switchable bank 1–7 |
| $E000 | $FDFF | Echo RAM (mirror of $C000–$DDFF) | Nintendo says use of this area is prohibited. |
| $FE00 | $FE9F | Object attribute memory (OAM) | |
| $FEA0 | $FEFF | Not Usable | Nintendo says use of this area is prohibited. |
| $FF00 | $FF7F | I/O Registers | |
| $FF80 | $FFEE | High RAM (HRAM) | |
| $FFFF | $FFFF | Interrupt Enable register (IE) | |

metadata.heading_path: Memory Map

text:
## I/O Ranges

The Game Boy uses the following I/O ranges:

| Start | End | First appeared | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| $FF00 | — | DMG | Joypad input (P1/JOYP) |
| $FF01–$FF02 | — | DMG | Serial transfer (Link Cable) |
| $FF04–$FF07 | — | DMG | Timer and divider (DIV, TIMA, TMA, TAC) |
| $FF0F | — | DMG | Interrupts (IF: Interrupt flag) |
| $FF10–$FF26 | — | DMG | Audio registers |
| $FF30–$FF3F | — | DMG | Wave pattern RAM |
| $FF40–$FF4B | — | DMG | LCD Control, Status, Position, Scrolling, and Palettes |
| $FF46 | — | DMG | OAM DMA transfer |
| $FF4C–$FF4D | — | CGB | KEY0 (CPU mode select) and KEY1 (Prepare speed switch) |
| $FF4F | — | CGB | VRAM Bank Select (VBK) |
| $FF50 | — | DMG | Boot ROM mapping control |
| $FF51–$FF55 | — | CGB | VRAM DMA |
| $FF56 | — | CGB | IR port (RP: Infrared communications port) |
| $FF68–$FF6B | — | CGB | BG / OBJ Palettes (CRAM) |
| $FF6C | — | CGB | Object priority mode (OPRI) |
| $FF70 | — | CGB | WRAM Bank Select (SVBK/WBK) |

metadata.heading_path: Memory Map > I/O Ranges

text:
## VRAM memory map

VRAM is normal RAM, but the PPU interprets it in specific ways.

Bank 1 does not exist except on CGB, where it can be switched to (only in CGB Mode) using the VBK register ($FF4F).

Each bank first contains 384 tiles, of 16 bytes each. These tiles are commonly thought of as grouped in three blocks of 128 tiles each.

The ID of a tile can be obtained from its address: ID = address / 16 mod 256. This is equivalent to only looking at the address' middle two hexadecimal digits.

After the tiles, each bank contains two maps, 32×32 (= 1024) bytes each. Bank 0 contains tile maps, while bank 1 contains the corresponding attribute maps (CGB Mode only).

Each map entry corresponds to coordinates linked to its address:
- X = address mod 32
- Y = address / 32 mod 32

The address of any entry can be thought of as a bitfield: bits 15-12 are $101, bit 11 selects tilemap (0 or 1), bits 9-5 are Y coordinate, bits 4-0 are X coordinate.

metadata.heading_path: Memory Map > VRAM memory map

text:
## Jump Vectors in first ROM bank

The following addresses are supposed to be used as jump vectors:

- RST instructions: $0000, $0008, $0010, $0018, $0020, $0028, $0030, $0038
- Interrupts: $0040, $0048, $0050, $0058, $0060

However, this memory area ($0000-$00FF) may be used for any other purpose if your program doesn't use any (or only some) rst instructions or interrupts. rst is a 1-byte instruction that works similarly to the 3-byte call instruction, except the destination address is restricted. Since it is 1-byte sized, it is also slightly faster.

metadata.section: Jump Vectors in first ROM bank
metadata.heading_path: Memory Map > Jump Vectors in first ROM bank

text:
## Cartridge Header in first ROM bank

The memory area at $0100-$014F contains the cartridge header. This area contains information about the program, its entry point, checksums, information about the used MBC chip, the ROM and RAM sizes, etc. Most of the bytes in this area are required to be specified correctly.

metadata.section: Cartridge Header in first ROM bank
metadata.heading_path: Memory Map > Cartridge Header in first ROM bank

text:
## External Memory and Hardware

The areas from $0000-$7FFF and $A000-$BFFF address external hardware on the cartridge, which is essentially an expansion board. Typically this is a ROM and SRAM or, more often, a Memory Bank Controller (MBC). The RAM area can be read from and written to normally; writes to the ROM area control the MBC.

Some MBCs allow mapping of other hardware into the RAM area in this way.

Cartridge RAM is often battery buffered to hold saved game positions, high score tables, and other information when the Game Boy is turned off.

metadata.heading_path: Memory Map > External Memory and Hardware

text:
## Echo RAM

The range $E000-$FDFF is mapped to WRAM, but only the lower 13 bits of the address lines are connected, with the upper bits on the upper bank set internally in the memory controller by a bank swap register. This causes the address to effectively wrap around. All reads and writes to this range have the same effect as reads and writes to $C000-$DDFF.

Nintendo prohibits developers from using this memory range. The behavior is confirmed on all official hardware. Some emulators (such as VisualBoyAdvance <1.8) don't emulate Echo RAM.

In some flash cartridges, echo RAM interferes with SRAM normally at $A000-$BFFF. Software can check if Echo RAM is properly emulated by writing to RAM (avoid values $00 and $FF) and checking if said value is mirrored in Echo RAM and not cart SRAM.

metadata.heading_path: Memory Map > Echo RAM

text:
## FEA0–FEFF range

Nintendo indicates use of this area is prohibited. This area returns $FF when OAM is blocked, and otherwise the behavior depends on the hardware revision.

- On DMG, MGB, SGB, and SGB2: reads during OAM block trigger OAM corruption. Reads otherwise return $00.
- On CGB revisions 0-D: this area is a unique RAM area, but is masked with a revision-specific value.
- On CGB revision E, AGB, AGS, and GBP: it returns the high nibble of the lower address byte twice (e.g., FFAx returns $AA, FFBx returns $BB).

metadata.heading_path: Memory Map > FEA0–FEFF range

text:
## IME: Interrupt master enable flag [write only]

IME is a flag internal to the CPU that controls whether any interrupt handlers are called, regardless of the contents of IE.
IME cannot be read in any way, and is modified by these instructions/events only:

- ei: Enables interrupt handling (IME := 1)
- di: Disables interrupt handling (IME := 0)
- reti: Enables interrupts and returns (same as ei immediately followed by ret)
- When an interrupt handler is executed: Disables interrupts before calling the interrupt handler

IME is unset (interrupts disabled) when the game starts running.

The effect of ei is delayed by one instruction. This means that ei followed immediately by di does not allow any interrupts between them. This interacts with the halt bug in an interesting way.

metadata.heading_path: Interrupts > IME: Interrupt master enable flag [write only]

text:
## FFFF — IE: Interrupt enable

Bit layout:
- Bit 0: VBlank (Read/Write) — Controls whether the VBlank interrupt handler ($0040) may be called.
- Bit 1: LCD (Read/Write) — Controls whether the LCD/STAT interrupt handler ($0048) may be called.
- Bit 2: Timer (Read/Write) — Controls whether the Timer interrupt handler ($0050) may be called.
- Bit 3: Serial (Read/Write) — Controls whether the Serial interrupt handler ($0058) may be called.
- Bit 4: Joypad (Read/Write) — Controls whether the Joypad interrupt handler ($0060) may be called.

## FF0F — IF: Interrupt flag

Bit layout (same as IE):
- Bit 0: VBlank — Controls whether the VBlank interrupt is being requested.
- Bit 1: LCD — Controls whether the LCD/STAT interrupt is being requested.
- Bit 2: Timer — Controls whether the Timer interrupt is being requested.
- Bit 3: Serial — Controls whether the Serial interrupt is being requested.
- Bit 4: Joypad — Controls whether the Joypad interrupt is being requested.

When an interrupt request signal changes from low to high, the corresponding bit in IF becomes set (e.g., bit 0 when PPU enters VBlank).

Any set bits in IF are only requesting an interrupt. The actual execution happens only if both IME and the corresponding IE bit are set; otherwise the interrupt waits until both allow it.

The CPU automatically sets and clears IF bits, but the user may write to IF to manually request or discard interrupts.

metadata.heading_path: Interrupts > FFFF — IE: Interrupt enable / FF0F — IF: Interrupt flag

text:
## Interrupt handling

When an interrupt is serviced:

1. The IF bit corresponding to this interrupt and the IME flag are reset by the CPU. The former acknowledges the interrupt, while the latter prevents further interrupts until re-enabled (typically via reti).
2. The corresponding interrupt handler is called — a regular call like `call <address>` (current PC pushed onto stack, then set to handler address).

Interrupt service routine timing:
1. Two wait states are executed (2 M-cycles; presumably CPU executes nops).
2. Current PC value is pushed onto the stack (2 more M-cycles).
3. PC is set to the handler address ($40, $48, $50, $58, or $60) (1 M-cycle).

The entire process lasts 5 M-cycles.

metadata.heading_path: Interrupts > Interrupt handling

text:
## Interrupt priorities

When more than one bit in IF is set (multiple interrupts requested at once), the interrupt with the highest priority is serviced first. This can happen when:
1. More than one interrupt request signal changed from low to high simultaneously.
2. Several interrupts were requested while IME/IE didn't allow servicing.
3. The user wrote a value with several bits set to IF.

Priorities follow the order of bits in IE and IF: Bit 0 (VBlank) has highest priority, Bit 4 (Joypad) has lowest priority.

metadata.heading_path: Interrupts > Interrupt priorities

text:
## Nested interrupt handling

The CPU automatically disables all other interrupts by setting IME=0 when it services an interrupt. Usually IME remains zero until the handler returns (setting IME=1 via reti).

However, if you want to allow servicing of other interrupts (of any priority) during execution of an interrupt handler, you may do so by using the ei instruction in the handler.

metadata.heading_path: Interrupts > Nested interrupt handling

text:
## INT $40 — VBlank interrupt

This interrupt is requested every time the Game Boy enters VBlank (Mode 1).

The VBlank interrupt occurs approximately 59.7 times a second on handheld Game Boy (DMG or CGB) or Game Boy Player, and approximately 61.1 times a second on Super Game Boy (SGB). This interrupt occurs at the beginning of the VBlank period (LY=144).

During this period video hardware is not using VRAM so it may be freely accessed. This period lasts approximately 1.1 milliseconds.

metadata.heading_path: Interrupt Sources > INT $40 — VBlank interrupt

text:
## INT $48 — STAT interrupt

There are various sources which can trigger this interrupt as described in the STAT register ($FF41).

The various STAT interrupt sources (modes 0-2 and LYC=LY) have their state (inactive=low, active=high) logically ORed into a shared STAT interrupt line if their respective enable bit is turned on.

A STAT interrupt is triggered by a rising edge (transition from low to high) on the STAT interrupt line.

**STAT blocking**: If a STAT interrupt source logically ORs the interrupt line high while it's already set high by another source, then there will be no low-to-high transition and so no interrupt will occur. For example, if interrupts are enabled for two consecutive modes such as Mode 0 and Mode 1, then no interrupt triggers for Mode 1 (since the STAT line won't go low between them).

**Using the STAT interrupt**: A popular use is to indicate when the PPU is about to redraw a given LCD line. This can dynamically control SCX/SCY ($FF43/$FF42) for special video effects. Example: set LYC to WY, enable LY=LYC interrupt, and have the handler disable objects — useful for hiding sprites behind text boxes.

metadata.heading_path: Interrupt Sources > INT $48 — STAT interrupt

text:
## INT $50 — Timer interrupt

The timer interrupt is requested every time that the timer overflows (that is, when TIMA exceeds $FF).

metadata.heading_path: Interrupt Sources > INT $50 — Timer interrupt

text:
## INT $58 — Serial interrupt

The serial interrupt is requested upon completion of a serial data transfer. In other words, eight serial clock cycles after starting a transfer (by setting SC bit 7), the incoming data will be in SB and the interrupt will be requested.

metadata.heading_path: Interrupt Sources > INT $58 — Serial interrupt

text:
## INT $60 — Joypad interrupt

The Joypad interrupt is requested when any of P1 bits 0-3 change from High to Low. This happens when a button is pressed (provided action/direction buttons are enabled by bit 5/4 respectively). Due to switch bounce, one or more High-to-Low transitions are usually produced per press.

**Using the joypad interrupt**: Useful for identifying button presses if only action (bit 5) or direction (bit 4) is selected, but not both. If both are selected and a bit is already held Low by an action button, pressing the corresponding direction button makes no difference. The Joypad interrupt's main purpose is to terminate the STOP (low power) standby state. GBA SP seems unaffected by switch bounce.

metadata.heading_path: Interrupt Sources > INT $60 — Joypad interrupt

text:
# Object Attribute Memory (OAM)

The Game Boy PPU can display up to 40 movable objects (sprites), each 8×8 or 8×16 pixels. Only ten objects can be displayed per scanline.

Object tiles have the same format as BG tiles, but are taken from tile blocks 0 and 1 at $8000-$8FFF with unsigned numbering.

Object attributes reside in OAM at $FE00-$FE9F. Each of the 40 entries consists of four bytes.

metadata.heading_path: Object Attribute Memory (OAM)

text:
## Byte 0 — Y Position

Y = Object's vertical position on the screen + 16.

Examples:
- Y=0: hides an object (on OAM area)
- Y=2: hides an 8×8 object but displays last two rows of an 8×16 object
- Y=16: displays at top of screen
- Y=144: displays an 8×16 object aligned with bottom of screen
- Y=152: displays an 8×8 object aligned with bottom of screen
- Y=154: displays first six rows of an object at bottom
- Y=160: hides an object (on LCD)

metadata.heading_path: Object Attribute Memory (OAM) > Byte 0 — Y Position

text:
## Byte 1 — X Position

X = Object's horizontal position on the screen + 8.

An off-screen value (X=0 or X>=168) hides the object, but the object still contributes to the limit of ten objects per scanline. This can cause objects later in OAM not to be drawn on that line.
A better way to hide an object is to set its Y-coordinate off-screen.

metadata.heading_path: Object Attribute Memory (OAM) > Byte 1 — X Position

text:
## Byte 2 — Tile Index

In 8×8 mode (LCDC bit 2 = 0): This byte specifies the object's tile index ($00-$FF). Unsigned value selecting a tile from $8000-$8FFF. In CGB Mode, could be in VRAM bank 0 or 1 depending on bit 3 of the next byte.

In 8×16 mode (LCDC bit 2 = 1): The memory area at $8000-$8FFF is still interpreted as 8×8 tiles where every 2 tiles form an object. This byte specifies the index of the first (top) tile. Hardware enforces: LSB of tile index is ignored; top tile is NN & $FE, bottom tile is NN | $01.

metadata.heading_path: Object Attribute Memory (OAM) > Byte 2 — Tile Index

text:
## Byte 3 — Attributes/Flags

Bit layout:
- Bit 7: Priority (0 = OBJ over BG; 1 = BG color indices 1-3 drawn over this OBJ)
- Bit 6: Y flip (0 = Normal; 1 = Vertically mirrored)
- Bit 5: X flip (0 = Normal; 1 = Horizontally mirrored)
- Bit 4: DMG palette [Non CGB Mode only] (0 = OBP0; 1 = OBP1)
- Bit 3: Bank [CGB Mode Only] (0 = VRAM bank 0; 1 = VRAM bank 1)
- Bits 2-0: CGB palette [CGB Mode Only] — Which of OBP0-7 to use

metadata.heading_path: Object Attribute Memory (OAM) > Byte 3 — Attributes/Flags

text:
## Writing data to OAM

The recommended method is to write data to a buffer in normal RAM (typically WRAM) first, then copy that buffer to OAM using the DMA transfer functionality.

Direct writes to OAM are possible by accessing it normally during HBlank and VBlank periods only.

metadata.heading_path: Object Attribute Memory (OAM) > Writing data to OAM

text:
## Object Priority and Conflicts

Two kinds of priorities exist for objects: selection priority (which objects are ignored when >10 on a scanline) and drawing priority (which overlapping object is displayed on top).

### Selection priority

During each scanline's OAM scan, the PPU compares LY to each object's Y position (using LCDC bit 2 for size) to select up to 10 objects. The PPU scans OAM sequentially from $FE00 to $FE9F, selecting the first (up to) 10 suitably-positioned objects.

Since only Y coordinate is checked, off-screen objects count toward the 10-object limit. Setting X=0 or X>=168 hides an object but it still counts. To keep off-screen objects from affecting on-screen ones, set their Y to Y=0 or Y>=160 (or Y<=8 if OBJ size is 8×8).

metadata.heading_path: Object Attribute Memory (OAM) > Object Priority and Conflicts > Selection priority

text:
### Drawing priority

When opaque pixels from two different objects overlap, the higher-priority object's pixel wins. Priority is determined differently by mode:

- **Non-CGB mode**: Smaller X coordinate = higher priority. When X coordinates are identical, the object first in OAM has higher priority.
- **CGB mode**: Only OAM location determines priority. Earlier object = higher priority.

**Interaction with BG over OBJ flag**: The PPU first resolves priority between objects to pick an object pixel (first non-transparent pixel iterating by drawing priority). The BG over OBJ attribute is never considered in this process. Only after object priority is resolved, the object pixel's BG over OBJ attribute determines whether it draws over background. A higher-priority object with BG over OBJ enabled will mask lower-priority objects even if those have BG over OBJ disabled.

In CGB Mode, BG vs OBJ priority is declared in more than one register; see BG-to-OBJ Priority in CGB Mode.

metadata.heading_path: Object Attribute Memory (OAM) > Object Priority and Conflicts > Drawing priority

text:
## FF04 — DIV: Divider register

This register is incremented at a rate of 16384 Hz (~16779 Hz on SGB). Writing any value to this register resets it to $00.

Additionally, DIV is reset when executing the stop instruction, and only begins ticking again once stop mode ends. This also occurs during a speed switch.

The divider is affected by CGB double speed mode, and will increment at 32768 Hz in double speed.

metadata.heading_path: Timer and Divider Registers > FF04 — DIV: Divider register

text:
## FF05 — TIMA: Timer counter

This timer is incremented at the clock frequency specified by TAC ($FF07). When it overflows (exceeds $FF), it resets to the value in TMA ($FF06) and a Timer interrupt ($0050) is requested.

## FF06 — TMA: Timer modulo

When TIMA overflows, it is reset to this register's value and an interrupt is requested.

Example: If TMA = $FF, an interrupt fires at the clock frequency selected in TAC (every increment is overflow). If TMA = $FE, interrupts fire every two increments (effectively dividing clock by 2). TMA = $FD divides by 3, and so on.

If a TMA write occurs on the same M-cycle as TIMA overflow transfers TMA to TIMA, the old value is transferred.

metadata.heading_path: Timer and Divider Registers > FF05 — TIMA / FF06 — TMA

text:
## FF07 — TAC: Timer control

Bit layout:
- Bit 2: Enable (controls whether TIMA is incremented; DIV always counts regardless)
- Bits 1-0: Clock select

Clock select frequencies:
| Select | Increment every | DMG/SGB2/CGB normal Hz | SGB1 Hz | CGB double speed Hz |
|--------|-----------------|----------------------|---------|--------------------|
| 00 | 256 M-cycles | 4096 | ~4194 | 8192 |
| 01 | 4 M-cycles | 262144 | ~268400 | 524288 |
| 10 | 16 M-cycles | 65536 | ~67110 | 131072 |
| 11 | 64 M-cycles | 16384 | ~16780 | 32768 |

Note: Writing to TAC may increase TIMA once (see Relation between Timer and Divider register).

metadata.heading_path: Timer and Divider Registers > FF07 — TAC: Timer control

text:
## FF46 — DMA: OAM DMA source address & start

Writing to this register starts a DMA transfer from ROM or RAM to OAM ($FE00-$FE9F). The written value specifies the source address divided by $100:

Source: $XX00-$XX9F (XX = $00 to $DF)
Destination: $FE00-$FE9F

The transfer takes 160 M-cycles: 640 dots (1.4 lines) in normal speed, or 320 dots (0.7 lines) in CGB Double Speed Mode. This is much faster than a CPU-driven copy.

metadata.section: FF46 — DMA: OAM DMA source address & start
metadata.heading_path: OAM DMA Transfer > FF46 — DMA

text:
## OAM DMA bus conflicts

On DMG, during OAM DMA, the CPU can access only HRAM ($FF80-$FFFE). The programmer must copy a short procedure into HRAM to start the transfer from inside HRAM and wait for completion.

On CGB, cartridge and WRAM are on separate buses. CPU can access ROM/SRAM during OAM DMA from WRAM, or WRAM during OAM DMA from ROM/SRAM. But since call writes return address to stack (usually in WRAM), it's still recommended to busy-wait in HRAM.

**Interrupts**: Critical to prevent interrupts during OAM DMA, especially timer/serial/joypad interrupts not synchronized to LCD. Do this via VBlank handler or di instruction.

While OAM DMA is active, the PPU cannot read OAM properly:
- During Mode 2 (OAM scan): most revisions read each object as off-screen and hidden.
- During Mode 3 (rendering): PPU reads whatever 16-bit word DMA writes to OAM, causing incorrect tile number and attributes.

Most programs execute DMA during Mode 1 (VBlank). Mid-frame DMA is possible for >40 objects but causes lines lacking objects.

metadata.heading_path: OAM DMA Transfer > OAM DMA bus conflicts

text:
## Best practices

Standard 10-byte HRAM routine to start DMA and wait for completion:

```rgbasm
run_dma:
    ld a, HIGH(start address)
    ldh [$FF46], a   ; start DMA transfer
    ld a, 40         ; delay for 4x40 = 160 M-cycles
.wait:
    dec a            ; 1 M-cycle
    jr nz, .wait     ; 3 M-cycles
    ret
```

Compact variant (saves 5 bytes HRAM):
```rgbasm
run_dma:          ; ROM part
    ld a, HIGH(start address)
    ld bc, $2846   ; B: wait time; C: LOW($FF46)
    jp run_dma_tail

run_dma_tail:     ; HRAM part
    ldh [c], a
.wait:
    dec b
    jr nz, .wait
    ret z          ; 1 M-cycle slower, avoids stack read on last DMA cycle
```

For mid-frame transfer: wait for Mode 0 first so the transfer cleanly overlaps Mode 2 on the next two lines.

metadata.heading_path: OAM DMA Transfer > Best practices

text:
# Rendering overview

## Terminology

The frame is not drawn atomically; the PPU (Pixel-Processing Unit) draws progressively, directly to the screen. A frame consists of 154 scanlines; during the first 144, the screen is drawn top to bottom, left to right.

Modifying rendering parameters mid-frame creates raster effects (e.g., modifying scroll registers between scanlines for wavy effects).

A dot = one 2^22 Hz (~4.194 MHz) time unit. Dots remain constant regardless of CPU speed mode: 4 dots per Normal Speed M-cycle, 2 per Double Speed M-cycle.

A frame is not exactly 1/60 second: the Game Boy runs slightly slower than 60 Hz (~16.74 ms per frame vs ~16.67 ms, error 0.45%).

metadata.heading_path: Rendering overview > Terminology

text:
## PPU modes

During a frame, the PPU cycles between four modes:

| Mode | Action | Duration | Accessible video memory |
|-----:|--------|----------|------------------------|
| 2 | Searching for OBJs which overlap this line | 80 dots | VRAM, CGB palettes |
| 3 | Sending pixels to the LCD | 172-289 dots (see below) | None |
| 0 | Waiting until end of scanline | 376 - mode 3's duration | VRAM, OAM, CGB palettes |
| 1 | Waiting until next frame (VBlank) | 4560 dots (10 scanlines) | VRAM, OAM, CGB palettes |

While the PPU is accessing video-related memory, that memory is inaccessible to the CPU (writes ignored, reads return garbage usually $FF).

metadata.heading_path: Rendering overview > PPU modes

text:
## Mode 3 length

Minimum Mode 3 is 172 dots (160 pixels + 12 dots for initial tile fetches). The Game Boy does not always output pixels steadily; features cause rendering to stall, lengthening Mode 3 and shortening Mode 0 by the same amount.

Three penalties:
- **Background scrolling**: SCX % 8 dots at start of Mode 3 while leftmost tile pixels are discarded.
- **Window**: 6-dot penalty after last non-window pixel while BG fetcher sets up for window.
- **Objects**: Each object drawn (even partially) incurs a 6- to 11-dot penalty.

On DMG and GBC in DMG mode, mid-scanline writes to BGP ($FF47) allow observing this behavior precisely.

### OBJ penalty algorithm

Only the OBJ's leftmost pixel matters. Steps:
1. Determine which tile (BG or window) The Pixel is within.
2. If that tile was not already considered by a previous OBJ: count pixels strictly right of The Pixel in that tile, subtract 2, incur that many dots (or zero if negative).
3. Incur flat 6-dot penalty for fetching the OBJ's tile.

Exception: An OBJ with OAM X position = 0 always incurs an 11-dot penalty regardless of SCX.

metadata.heading_path: Rendering overview > Mode 3 length

text:
# Joypad Input

## FF00 — P1/JOYP: Joypad

The eight Game Boy action/direction buttons are arranged as a 2×4 matrix. Select either action or direction buttons by writing to this register, then read bits 0-3.

Bit layout:
- Bit 5: Select buttons (0 = buttons readable from lower nibble)
- Bit 4: Select d-pad (0 = directional keys readable from lower nibble)
- Bits 3-0: Read-only button states
  - Bit 3: Start / Down
  - Bit 2: Select / Up
  - Bit 1: B / Left
  - Bit 0: A / Right

A button being pressed is seen as the corresponding bit being 0 (not 1). If neither buttons nor d-pad selected ($30 written), low nibble reads $F.

Most programs read this port several times in a row; first reads serve as delay for input stabilization, only last read value is used.

## Usage in SGB software

SGB games misuse the joypad register to output SGB command packets to SNES. SGB programs may also read gamepad states from up to four SNES-connected joypads.

metadata.heading_path: Joypad Input > FF00 — P1/JOYP: Joypad

text:
# Graphics Overview

The Game Boy outputs graphics to a 160×144 pixel LCD using a complex rendering mechanism.

## Tiles

Pixels are grouped in 8×8 squares called tiles (or patterns/characters), the base unit of Game Boy graphics. A tile does not encode color information; instead, it assigns color indices (0-3) to each pixel. This makes Game Boy graphics 2bpp (2 bits per pixel).

When a tile is used in Background or Window, color indices are associated with a palette. When used in an object, indices 1-3 use a palette but ID 0 means transparent.

metadata.heading_path: Graphics Overview > Tiles

text:
## Palettes

A palette consists of an array of 4 colors. Modifying palettes enables effects like flashing (damage, invulnerability), fading, and palette swaps.

## Layers

The Game Boy has three layers from back to front: Background, Window, and Objects.

**Background**: Composed of a tilemap — a large grid containing references to tiles. Tiles are reused cheaply in CPU time and memory. The background scrolls as a whole via two hardware registers.

**Window**: A second background layer on top of the background. No transparency; always rectangular; only top-left position controllable. Used for fixed status bars (e.g., Super Mario Land 2).

**Objects**: Made of 1 or 2 stacked tiles (8×8 or 8×16 pixels), displayed anywhere on screen. Multiple objects can be combined as metasprites to draw larger graphical elements.

Summary: A Tile is an 8×8-pixel chunk. An Object is an OAM entry composed of 1-2 tiles, movable independently.

metadata.heading_path: Graphics Overview > Palettes / Layers

text:
# VRAM Tile Data

Tile data is stored in VRAM at $8000-$97FF; each tile takes 16 bytes, defining 384 tiles. In CGB Mode this doubles to 768 tiles (two VRAM banks).

Each tile has 8×8 pixels with 2 bits per pixel (4 colors/shades). Color 0 is transparent in objects.

Three blocks of 128 tiles:
| Tile IDs for... | Block 0 ($8000-$87FF) | Block 1 ($8800-$8FFF) | Block 2 ($9000-$97FF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Objects | 0-127 | 128-255 | — |
| BG/Win, LCDC.4=1 | 0-127 | 128-255 | — |
| BG/Win, LCDC.4=0 | — | 128-255 | 0-127 |

**$8000 method**: Unsigned addressing; tiles 0-127 in block 0, tiles 128-255 in block 1.
**$8800 method**: Signed addressing; tiles 0-127 from block 2, tiles 128-255 (i.e., -128 to -1) from block 1.

Objects always use $8000 addressing. BG/Window mode controlled by LCDC bit 4.

metadata.heading_path: VRAM Tile Data > Addressing methods

text:
## Data format

Each tile occupies 16 bytes; each row is represented by 2 bytes (topmost line first).

For each row, the first byte specifies the least significant bit of each pixel's color ID, and the second byte specifies the most significant bit. In both bytes, bit 7 = leftmost pixel, bit 0 = rightmost.

Example: Row data $3C $7E = binary 00111100 and 01111110. Leftmost bits are 0+0 → color index 0. Next bits 0+1 → color index 2 (MSB first!). Full row evaluates to: 0 2 3 3 3 3 2 0.

Each pixel has a color index of 0-3, translated by palettes (BGP $FF47, OBP0/OBP1 $FF48/$FF49 for DMG; BCPS/BCPD/OCPS/OCPD for CGB). In OBJs, color index 0 is transparent.

metadata.heading_path: VRAM Tile Data > Data format
keywords: LSB byte first MSB byte second

text:
# Accessing VRAM and OAM

**Warning**: When the PPU is drawing the screen, it reads directly from VRAM and OAM. During these periods, the CPU cannot access them.

Writes to VRAM/OAM during PPU access are ignored (data unchanged). Reads return undefined data (typically $FF).

The program should verify if VRAM/OAM is accessible before reading/writing, usually by checking Mode bits in STAT ($FF41). When doing so, ensure no interrupts occur between the wait loop and the memory access; accessibility is guaranteed only for a few cycles after a wait loop exits.

metadata.heading_path: Accessing VRAM and OAM > Warning

text:
## VRAM ($8000-$9FFF) is accessible during Modes 0-2

Mode 0 (HBlank), Mode 1 (VBlank), and Mode 2 (OAM search) allow VRAM access.

Typical wait procedure:
```rgbasm
    ld hl, $FF41   ; STAT Register
.wait:
    bit 1, [hl]    ; Wait until Mode is 0 or 1
    jr nz, .wait
```

Even at end of Mode 0/1, VRAM access is safe because the next period is Mode 2 (also allows VRAM). But be careful about STAT interrupts that could return during Mode 3.

In CGB Mode, HDMA ($FF51-$FF55) provides an alternate method to write VRAM data.

Alternative synchronization: Enable only Mode 0 STAT interrupt (STAT bit 3), enable STAT in IE (bit 1), execute di, then halt. This uses entire Mode 0 + following Mode 2 (165-288 dots total).

metadata.heading_path: Accessing VRAM and OAM > VRAM ($8000-$9FFF) is accessible during Modes 0-2

text:
## OAM ($FE00-$FE9F) is accessible during Modes 0-1

Mode 0 (HBlank) and Mode 1 (VBlank) allow OAM access.

During those modes, OAM can be accessed directly or via DMA transfer ($FF46). Outside those modes, DMA out-prioritizes the PPU in accessing OAM; the PPU reads $FF from OAM during that time.

Typical wait procedure:
```rgbasm
    ld hl, $FF41   ; STAT Register
.waitNotBlank:
    bit 1, [hl]
    jr z, .waitNotBlank   ; Wait until mode is NOT 0 or 1
.waitBlank:
    bit 1, [hl]
    jr nz, .waitBlank     ; Wait until Mode 0 or 1 begins
```

The two wait loops ensure Mode 0 (and Mode 1 at frame end) lasts long enough after the procedure. For VBlank waiting, a STAT interrupt is better. DMA transfer is more efficient than direct OAM writes.

While display is disabled, both VRAM and OAM are fully accessible.

metadata.heading_path: Accessing VRAM and OAM > OAM ($FE00-$FE9F) is accessible during Modes 0-1

text:
# Viewport position (Scrolling)

## FF42–FF43 — SCY, SCX: Background viewport Y position, X position

These registers can be accessed even during Mode 3, but modifications may not take effect immediately.

SCY and SCX specify the top-left coordinates of the visible 160×144 pixel area within the 256×256 pixel BG map. Values range 0-255.

The PPU calculates bottom-right viewport coordinates: bottom = (SCY + 143) % 256, right = (SCX + 159) % 256. Values larger than 255 wrap around toward the top-left corner of the tilemap.

## Mid-frame behavior

Scroll registers are re-read on each tile fetch, except for the low 3 bits of SCX, which are only read at scanline start (for initial pixel shifting).

All models before CGB-D read the Y coordinate once per bitplane (allowing desync with precisely timed SCY writes). CGB-D and later use the same Y coordinate for both bitplanes.

metadata.heading_path: Viewport position (Scrolling) > FF42-FF43 — SCY, SCX

text:
## FF4A–FF4B — WY, WX: Window Y position, X position plus 7

These registers specify the on-screen coordinates of the Window's top-left pixel.

The Window is visible (if enabled) when WX and WY are in range [0; 166] and [0; 143] respectively. Values WX=7, WY=0 place the Window at top-left, completely covering the background.

metadata.heading_path: Window behavior > FF4A-FF4B — WY, WX

text:
## Window mid-frame behavior

For least glitchy results, only write to WX, WY, and LCDC during VBlank. If mid-frame writes are required, prefer HBlank.

To hide the Window for part of the screen (e.g., top status bar), set WX to a high value rather than writing to LCDC.

### Window rendering criteria

The PPU tracks a Y condition throughout a frame:
- On each VBlank, the Y condition is cleared (false).
- At scanline start, if WY equals LY, the Y condition becomes true (remains so for subsequent scanlines).

On GBC, clearing Window enable bit in LCDC resets the Y condition; WY must be set to LY or greater for the Window to display again.

The PPU maintains a counter initialized to 0 at scanline start, incremented per pixel rendered. It also increments 7 times before first pixel (covering fine scroll discard).

When counter equals WX, if Y condition is true and Window enable bit set in LCDC, background rendering resets from the active row of Window's tilemap.

- This can happen more than once per scanline (requires disabling Window via LCDC first).
- If not triggered, Window's tilemap Y coordinate doesn't increase; hiding then showing produces vertical striped stretching.
- WX=0: Window switches before fine scroll adjustment, shifted left by SCX % 8 pixels.
- On monochrome systems, WX=166 has a bug: Window spans entire screen but offset vertically by one scanline.
- On monochrome systems, if Window disabled via LCDC but conditions met and would start exactly on BG tile boundary, a single pixel with ID 0 is inserted, offsetting the remainder of the scanline.

metadata.heading_path: Window behavior > Window mid-frame behavior
keywords: WX WY mid-frame write VBlank HBlank
GBC LCDC window enable reset Y condition
LCDC disabled tile boundary pixel ID 0

text:
# Pixel FIFO

## Introduction

FIFO (First In, First Out): first pixel pushed is first popped. There are two independent pixel FIFOs — one for background pixels and one for object (sprite) pixels.

Each FIFO holds up to 16 pixels. The FIFO and Pixel Fetcher ensure at least 8 pixels are always present (required for Pixel Rendering). Each FIFO is manipulated only during Mode 3.

Each pixel in the FIFO has four properties:
- Color: value 0-3
- Palette: CGB value 0-7; DMG applies only to objects
- Sprite Priority: CGB = OAM index; DMG doesn't exist
- Background Priority: value of OBJ-to-BG Priority bit

metadata.heading_path: Pixel FIFO > Introduction

text:
## FIFO Pixel Fetcher

The fetcher fetches a row of 8 background/window pixels. It has 5 steps: first four take 2 dots each, fifth is attempted every dot until success.

Steps: Get Tile → Get Tile Data Low → Get Tile Data High → Sleep → Push

### Get Tile

Determines which BG/Window tile to fetch from. Default tilemap is $9800.
- When LCDC.3 enabled and X not in window: tilemap $9C00 used.
- When LCDC.6 enabled and X inside window: tilemap $9C00 used.

Fetcher tracks X/Y coordinates:
- Window tile: uses window X coordinate directly.
- BG tile: X = ((SCX / 8) + fetcherX) & $1F (range 0-31).
- Y for BG: (currentScanline + SCY) & 255 (range 0-255).

If VRAM access is blocked, tile value reads as $FF. CGB can access both tile index and attributes in the same clock dot.

metadata.heading_path: Pixel FIFO > FIFO Pixel Fetcher > Get Tile

text:
### Get Tile Data Low

Check LCDC.4 for tilemap. CGB also checks VRAM bank and vertical flip. Retrieves tile data from VRAM (reads $FF if blocked).

### Get Tile Data High

Same as Low except address incremented by 1. Also pushes a row of BG/Window pixels to FIFO (3 total push chances per complete fetcher cycle).

### Push

Pushes 8 pixels to background FIFO only if empty. Horizontal flip determines LSB-first vs MSB-first push order.

### Sleep

Do nothing.

### VRAM Access

VRAM read blocked (returns $FF) during: LCD turning off, scanline 0 on CGB not double speed, mode 3→0 transition, CGB OAM search index 37.

VRAM read restored at: scanline 0 DMG/CGB double speed, DMG OAM search index 37, mode 2→3 transition. Conditions checked only when entering STOP; always restored leaving STOP.

metadata.section: Get Tile Data Low/High, Push, Sleep, VRAM Access
metadata.heading_path: Pixel FIFO > FIFO Pixel Fetcher > Get Tile Data / Push / VRAM Access
keywords: VRAM blocked LCD off scanline 0 mode transition OAM index 37

text:
## Mode 3 Operation

At beginning of Mode 3, both BG and OAM FIFOs are cleared.

### The Window

When rendering window: BG FIFO cleared, fetcher reset to step 1. WX=0 with SCX & 7 > 0 shortens mode 3 by 1 dot.

Bug: Changing WX mid-scanline after window started rendering pushes a color-0 lowest-priority pixel when new WX value is reached again.

### Sprites

For each object on current scanline (if LCDC.1 enabled — ignored on CGB):
- Fetcher advances one step until at step 5 or BG FIFO not empty (+1 dot per advance). May be canceled.
- SCX & 7 > 0 with object at X=0 lengthens mode 3 by lower 3 bits of SCX dots. Timing unconfirmed.
- After checking X=0, fetcher advances two steps (first +1 dot, second +3 dots).
- Lower address retrieved (+1 dot). Upper address retrieved (no penalty).
- VRAM Access checked for both addresses.
- If OAM FIFO has <8 pixels, transparent lowest-priority pixels pushed.
- Each pixel checked: on CGB horizontal flip checked here. Non-white target replaces white OAM pixel, or higher priority replaces lower.

text:
### Pixel Rendering

BG and OAM FIFOs are mixed. If both have pixels, one popped from each.

If OAM pixel not transparent and LCDC.1 enabled: OAM pixel's BG priority property is used if same or higher than BG pixel's BG priority.

Pixels won't push to LCD if BG FIFO empty or pixel >= 160.

LCDC.0 disabled: BG disabled on DMG, no BG priority on CGB. Disabled BG pixel color = 0. Non-zero BG pixel with priority discards object pixel.

On DMG: BG pixel color from BGP register pushed to LCD. On CGB when palette access blocked: black pixel pushed.

Object pixel priority: Color from OAM FIFO popped pixel. DMG uses OBP1 (palette=1) or OBP0 (palette=0). CGB palette blocked → black pixel.

### CGB Palette Access

Blocked (black pixel): LCD off, first HBlank frame, OAM search index 37, mode 2→3 transition, entering HBlank not double speed (blocked 2 dots later).

Restored: End of mode 2, 2 dots entering HBlank in double speed. Checked only when entering STOP; always restored leaving STOP.

text:
### Object Fetch Canceling

Object fetching may be canceled if LCDC.1 is disabled while PPU fetches an object from OAM.

Canceling lengthens Mode 3 by: dots of previous instruction + residual dots for PPU processing.

When OAM fetching is canceled: a pixel is rendered, and the fetcher advances one step (+1 dot if current pixel < 160). At pixel 160, PPU stops processing objects (not visible).

text:
# OAM Corruption Bug

A hardware flaw causes rubbish data written to OAM when certain instructions are used while their 16-bit content (before operation) is in range $FE00-$FEFF and PPU is in Mode 2:

Affected instructions: inc rr, dec rr (rr = bc, de, hl); ld a,[hli], ld a,[hld]; ld [hli],a, ld [hld],a.

Objects 0 and 1 ($FE00 & $FE04) are not affected. Game Boy Color and Advance are not affected, even running monochrome software.

metadata.heading_path: OAM Corruption Bug > Overview
keywords: inc rr dec rr bc de hl

text:
## Accurate Description

The OAM Bug consists of two different bugs:
1. Reading/writing OAM (including $FEA0-$FEFF) during Mode 2 corrupts it.
2. Increment/decrement on any 16-bit register (BC, DE, HL, SP, PC) while in OAM range ($FE00-$FEFF) triggers an access because the CPU's IDU is directly tied to the address bus.

## Affected Operations

- Any memory access instruction accessing OAM.
- inc rr, dec rr: if 16-bit register points to OAM, triggers write corruption.
- ld [hli],a etc.: trigger corruption twice (memory access + inc/dec).
- pop rr, ret family: bug triggers 3 times (one read, one glitched write, another read without glitched write).
- push rr, call family, rst xx, interrupt handling: 4 triggers (two writes + two glitched dec sp writes; effectively 3 writes since one overlaps).
- Executing code from OAM: PC inside OAM ($FF = rst $38) triggers twice. Multi-byte opcode from $FDFF/$FDFE similarly triggers twice per read.

metadata.heading_path: OAM Corruption Bug > Accurate Description / Affected Operations
keywords: IDU increment decrement unit address bus tied
push call rst interrupt 4 triggers dec sp

text:
## Corruption Patterns

OAM is split into 20 rows of 8 bytes. During Mode 2, PPU reads rows consecutively (one per M-cycle). OAM uses a 16-bit data bus; all operations are on 16-bit words.

### Write Corruption

Corrupts the currently accessed row (except first row with objects 0-1):
- First word replaced: ((a ^ c) & (b ^ c)) ^ c, where a = original value, b = first word preceding row, c = third word preceding row.
- Last three words copied from last three words of preceding row.

### Read Corruption

Same as write but expression is: b | (a & c).

### Write During Increase/Decrease

Two writes in one M-cycle behave like a single write.

### Read During Increase/Decrease

Triggers both read AND write. Does not affect first four rows or last row:
- First word of preceding row replaced: (b & (a | c | d)) | (a & c & d), where a = first word two rows before, b = first word preceding row, c = first word current row, d = third word preceding row.
- Preceding row contents copied to current row and two rows before. Then normal read corruption applied.

metadata.heading_path: OAM Corruption Bug > Corruption Patterns
keywords: write corruption XOR formula a^c b^c
read corruption OR AND formula b|a&c

text:
# VRAM Tile Maps

The Game Boy contains two 32×32 tile maps in VRAM at $9800-$9BFF and $9C00-$9FFF. Any of these can display the Background or Window.

## Tile Indexes

Each map contains 1-byte indexes of tiles to display. Tiles are obtained from Tile Data using either addressing mode (selected via LCDC register). Since one tile is 8×8 pixels, each map holds a 256×256 pixel picture; only 160×144 displayed on LCD at any time.

metadata.heading_path: VRAM Tile Maps > Tile Indexes

text:
## BG Map Attributes (CGB Mode only)

In CGB Mode, an additional 32×32 byte map is stored in VRAM Bank 1 (each byte defines attributes for the corresponding tile-number map entry in VRAM Bank 0):

Bit layout:
- Bit 7: Priority (0 = No; 1 = BG/Window color indices 1-3 drawn over OBJ regardless of OBJ priority)
- Bit 6: Y flip (0 = Normal; 1 = Vertically mirrored)
- Bit 5: X flip (0 = Normal; 1 = Horizontally mirrored)
- Bit 4: Ignored by hardware
- Bit 3: Bank (0 = VRAM bank 0; 1 = VRAM bank 1)
- Bits 2-0: Color palette (BGP0-7)

### BG-to-OBJ Priority in CGB Mode

Priority between BG and OBJ is declared in three places: BG Map Attribute bit 7, LCDC bit 0, OAM Attributes bit 7.

Rules:
- If BG color index = 0: OBJ always has priority.
- If LCDC bit 0 clear: OBJ always has priority.
- If both BG attr and OAM attr bit 7 clear: OBJ has priority.
- Otherwise: BG has priority (for colors 1-3; color 0 still OBJ).

Note: OAM Attributes bit 7 grants OBJ priority when CLEAR, not SET. Priority between all OBJs is resolved before BG layer priority.

metadata.section: BG Map Attributes and BG-to-OBJ Priority
metadata.heading_path: VRAM Tile Maps > BG Map Attributes (CGB Mode only) / BG-to-OBJ Priority
keywords: CGB Mode VRAM Bank 1 attribute map
OAM attr bit 7 clear OBJ priority

text:
## Background (BG)

SCY and SCX registers scroll the Background, specifying origin of visible 160×144 area within total 256×256 BG map. Visible area wraps around the map.

In Non-CGB mode, Background (and Window) can be disabled using LCDC bit 0.

## Window

The Window overlays the Background. Content is not scrollable; always displayed from top-left tile of its tilemap. Position adjusted via WX and WY registers; top-left corner at screen coordinates (WX-7, WY).

Window display toggled by LCDC bit 5. In Non-CGB mode, this only works if LCDC bit 0 is set.
Enabling Window makes Mode 3 slightly longer on visible scanlines.

**Window Internal Line Counter**: The window keeps an internal line counter similar to LY, incrementing alongside it. It only increments when the window is visible (per Window rendering criteria). This determines what window line renders on each scanline.

text:
# Audio Overview

Game Boy audio has four sound generation units called channels 1-4 (CH1-CH4). Each channel is specialized differently.

Channels 1 and 2: Pulse channels producing pulse width modulated waves with 4 fixed duty settings.
Channel 3: Wave channel producing arbitrary user-supplied waves from Wave RAM.
Channel 4: Noise channel producing pseudo-random wave via LFSR.
VIN channel: Analog signal from cartridge for external hardware (no licensed games used it; omitted from GBA).

Each channel has components:
| Channel | Sweep | Frequency | Wave Form | Length Timer | Volume |
|---------|-------|-----------|-----------|-------------|--------|
| Square 1 | Sweep | Period Counter | Duty | Length Timer | Envelope |
| Square 2 | — | Period Counter | Duty | Length Timer | Envelope |
| Wave | — | Period Counter | Wave | Length Timer | Volume |
| Noise | — | Period Counter | LFSR | Length Timer | Envelope |

metadata.heading_path: Audio Overview > Architecture
keywords: CH2 pulse duty envelope no sweep

text:
## Common concepts

### APU
The sound chip (APU) runs off the same Master Clock as CPU and PPU. SGB1 runs ~2.4% faster (higher pitch); SGB2 rectifies this. APU tick length is not affected by CGB double speed.

All interfaces use durations instead of frequencies. E.g., "256 Hz tick" = 1/256th second.

### Triggering
Triggering a channel turns it on (if DAC not off) and starts playing from beginning. Most parameter changes take effect immediately, but some require re-triggering.

### Volume & envelope
Master volume control has separate left/right settings. Each channel's volume individually set. Envelope for CH1/CH2/CH4: initial volume, direction (not slope), duration. All envelopes tick at 64 Hz; volume changes every 1-7 ticks.

### Length timer
All channels can auto-shutdown after a set time. If enabled, length timer ticks at 256 Hz from initial value. Reaches 64 (CH1/2/4) or 256 (CH3) → channel off.

### Frequency
APU works with periods (negative values), not frequencies. Higher period value = lower period = higher frequency.

text:
# Audio Registers

Registers follow NRxy naming: x = channel (1-4) or 5 for global, y = register ID within channel. NRx0 = channel feature, NRx1 = length timer, NRx2 = volume/envelope, NRx3 = period low, NRx4 = trigger/length enable/period high.

### FF26 — NR52: Audio master control
- Bit 7: Audio on/off. Turning APU off drains ~16% less power but clears all registers (read-only until back on except NR52). Does NOT affect Wave RAM or DIV-APU counter.
- Bits 3-0: CH4/3/2/1 on? (Read-only status; writing does NOT enable/disable channels).

Channel turns off when: length timer expires, CH1 period sweep overflows, or DAC turned off. Envelope reaching volume 0 does NOT turn channel off.

### FF25 — NR51: Sound panning
Bits 7-4: CH4/3/2/1 left output. Bits 3-0: CH4/3/2/1 right output. Setting bit = 1 enables channel to that output. Selecting/deselecting with DAC enabled causes audio pop.

### FF24 — NR50: Master volume & VIN panning
Bit 7: VIN left, Bit 3: VIN right (set to 0 if no external hardware). Bits 6-4: Left volume, Bits 2-0: Right volume. Value 0 = volume 1 (quietest), value 7 = volume 8 (no reduction). Amplifier never mutes non-silent input.

text:
### FF10 — NR10: Channel 1 sweep
Controls CH1's period sweep. Bit layout:
- Bits 6-4: Pace (how often sweep iterations happen, in 128 Hz ticks / 7.8 ms). Value 0 disables iterations instantly.
- Bit 3: Direction (0 = Addition/period increases; 1 = Subtraction/period decreases)
- Bits 2-0: Individual step — new period L(t+1) = Lt ± Lt/(2^step)

On each iteration, the period in NR13/NR14 is modified and written back. In addition mode, if period > $7FF, channel turns off (even with pace=0). Period 0 cannot be changed by sweep; sweep cannot underflow.

### FF11 — NR11: Channel 1 length timer & duty cycle
- Bits 7-6: Wave duty (00 = 12.5%, 01 = 25%, 10 = 50%, 11 = 75%). No audible difference between 25% and 75%.
- Bits 5-0: Initial length timer (higher value = shorter time before channel cut).

metadata.section: CH1 NR10 sweep and NR11 duty cycle

text:
### FF12 — NR12: Channel 1 volume & envelope
Controls digital amplitude and sweep applied to it.
- Bits 7-4: Initial volume (readable but NOT updated by envelope)
- Bit 3: Env dir (0 = decrease; 1 = increase)
- Bits 2-0: Sweep pace (envelope ticks at 64 Hz; changes every N ticks). Value 0 disables envelope.

Setting bits 3-7 all to 0 turns DAC off. Writes while channel is on require retriggering.

### FF13 — NR13: Channel 1 period low [write-only]
Low 8 bits of 11-bit period value. Upper 3 bits in low 3 bits of NR14.

Period divider is an up counter clocked at 1048576 Hz (once per 4 dots). Waveform is 8 samples long.
Tone frequency = 131072 / (2048 - period_value) Hz. Period value treated as negative number in 11-bit two's complement.

Period changes only take effect after current sample ends.

### FF14 — NR14: Channel 1 period high & control
- Bit 7: Trigger (enables CH1, resets length/period divider/envelope/volume/sweep)
- Bit 6: Length enable (takes effect immediately)
- Bits 2-0: Period upper 3 bits

text:
## Sound Channel 2 — Pulse
Works exactly like CH1 except no period sweep (no NR10 equivalent).
Register mapping: NR21 ($FF16) → NR11, NR22 ($FF17) → NR12, NR23 ($FF18) → NR13, NR24 ($FF19) → NR14.

## Sound Channel 3 — Wave output
Allows arbitrary waveforms from Wave RAM (32 samples, 4-bit each). Can be shortened with repeating patterns or artificially lengthened for sample playback.

### FF1A — NR30: Channel 3 DAC enable
Bit 7: DAC on/off. Turning off immediately turns channel off. Often turned off before writing to Wave RAM. May cause audio pop.

### FF1B — NR31: Channel 3 length timer [write-only]
Bits 7-0: Initial length timer (higher = shorter time).

### FF1C — NR32: Channel 3 output level
No envelope; coarser volume control:
- Bits 6-5 = 00: Mute
- 01: 100% volume
- 10: 50% (shift right once)
- 11: 25% (shift right twice)

text:
### FF1D — NR33: Channel 3 period low [write-only]
Low 8 bits of 11-bit period value. Upper 3 bits in NR34.

Wave channel period divider clocked at 2097152 Hz (once per 2 dots). Waveform is 32 samples long.
Tone frequency = 65536 / (2048 - period_value) Hz. Same period value produces half the pulse channel frequency (one octave lower).

Period changes take effect after next wave RAM read.

### FF1E — NR34: Channel 3 period high & control
- Bit 7: Trigger (enables CH3, resets length/period divider/volume; resets Wave RAM index but NOT refills it)
- Bit 6: Length enable
- Bits 2-0: Period upper 3 bits

Retriggering CH3 while about to read wave RAM on monochrome consoles corrupts wave RAM unpredictably.
Triggering does not immediately start playing; last sample (reset to 0 when APU off) outputs until next read.

### FF30–FF3F — Wave pattern RAM
16 bytes, each holding two 4-bit samples. CH3 reads left-to-right, upper nibble first. First sample read on start is index 1 (lower nibble of $FF30), NOT upper nibble.

Accessing wave RAM while CH3 active causes misbehavior: AGB returns $FF/ignores writes; monochrome only accessible on same cycle as CH3; other consoles access whatever byte CH3 reads. Wave RAM accessible normally if DAC on but channel not active.

text:
## Sound Channel 4 — Noise
Outputs white noise by randomly switching amplitude between two levels. Frequency adjustable for harder/softer sound. LFSR settings can make output more regular (closer to pulse than noise).

### FF20 — NR41: Channel 4 length timer [write-only]
Bits 5-0: Initial length timer (higher = shorter time).

### FF21 — NR42: Channel 4 volume & envelope
Functions exactly like NR12 ($FF12). Refer to CH1 documentation.

### FF22 — NR43: Channel 4 frequency & randomness
- Bits 7-4: Clock shift. Shift = 14 or 15 stops clocking entirely.
- Bit 3: LFSR width (0 = 15-bit; 1 = 7-bit, more regular). Switching 15→7 bit in certain state locks LFSR (silences CH4); retrigger to reset.
- Bits 2-0: Clock divider. Divider = 0 treated as 0.5.

LFSR clock frequency = 262144 / (divider × 2^shift) Hz. If shifted-out bit is 0, output 0; otherwise outputs NR42 volume.

### FF23 — NR44: Channel 4 control
- Bit 7: Trigger (enables CH4, resets length/envelope/volume/LFSR)
- Bit 6: Length enable

text:
# Audio Details

The APU is a collection of counters. Each of the four conceptual channels has a generation circuit and a DAC. The digital value ($0-$F, 0-15) is linearly translated by the DAC into an analog value between -1 and 1.

The four analog outputs are fed into the mixer, which selectively adds them (depending on NR51) into two analog outputs (Left/Right). Range of these outputs: -4 to 4. Both get volume scaled from NR50 and then from the hardware volume knob. The former never mutes a non-silent input; the latter can.

Each output goes through a high-pass filter (HPF) that constantly tries to pull the signal towards analog 0, removing DC offset from inactive channels with enabled DACs and off-center waveforms.

## PCM Registers (CGB Mode only)

Two undocumented registers allow reading generation circuit outputs directly:
- $FF76 — PCM12: Low nibble = CH1 digital output; high nibble = CH2 digital output. Read-only.
- $FF77 — PCM34: Same for channels 3 and 4. Read-only.

text:
## DIV-APU Counter

A DIV-APU counter increments every time DIV's bit 4 (bit 5 in double-speed mode) goes from 1 to 0, at a frequency of 512 Hz regardless of double-speed. Writing to DIV while the relevant bit is set triggers the falling edge, making the counter increase faster.

Events per N DIV-APU ticks:
| Event | Rate | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| Envelope sweep | 8 | 64 Hz |
| Sound length | 2 | 256 Hz |
| CH1 freq sweep | 4 | 128 Hz |

## Mixer and HPF Details

The HPF removes constant biases over time, eliminating DC offset from inactive channels with enabled DACs and off-center waveforms.

**Avoiding audio pops**: Enabling/disabling a DAC, adding/removing via NR51, or changing volume in NR50 causes an audio pop (DC offset change smoothed by HPFs over time). To avoid: write $08 to NRx2 (silences channel but keeps DAC on), then $80 to NRx4 to retrigger and reload NRx2.

HPF aggressiveness varies by model: GBA > GBC > DMG. More aggressive = faster pull toward analog 0, which also distorts waveforms more.

text:
## DACs

Channel x's DAC is enabled iff `[NRx2] & $F8 != 0`. Exception: CH3's DAC controlled directly by bit 7 of NR30. Envelope changes volume but not the stored NRx2 value, so doesn't disable DACs.

When enabled: digital $0-$F linearly translated to analog -1 to 1 with NEGATIVE slope (digital 0 maps to analog 1). When disabled: fades to analog 0 (= digital 7.5); fade nature varies between models and is not fully deterministic.

NR52's low 4 bits report whether channels are on, NOT their DACs.

## Channels Activation/Deactivation

A channel activates via write to NRx4's MSB (trigger), unless its DAC is off — then it stays disabled. A disabled channel outputs digital 0, which an enabled DAC converts to analog 1.

Channel deactivation methods:
- Turning off its DAC
- Length timer expiring
- CH1 only: frequency sweep overflowing the period

text:
## Pulse Channel with Sweep (CH1) Detailed Behavior

CH1's frequency sweep unit (NR10) has a sweep timer, enabled flag, and period shadow register. The sweep timer is clocked at 128 Hz by DIV-APU.

**Trigger event for CH1:**
- Period value copied to shadow register
- Sweep timer reset
- Enabled flag set if sweep pace OR individual step are non-zero; cleared otherwise
- If individual step non-zero: frequency calculation AND overflow check performed immediately

**Frequency calculation**: Take shadow register value, shift right by individual step, optionally negate (per direction bit), sum with shadow register to produce new frequency.

**Overflow check**: New frequency > 2047 ($7FF) → CH1 disabled.

**Sweep timer clocked** (when enabled flag set and pace non-zero): Calculate new frequency + overflow check. If ≤ 2047 and step non-zero: write to shadow register AND NR13/NR14, then run calculation+overflow AGAIN immediately with the new value (second result NOT written back).

Modifying CH1 frequency via NR13/NR14 while sweep active doesn't affect shadow register; next sweep update overwrites it. Re-triggering avoids this.

text:
## Pulse Channels (CH1, CH2) Duty Step

Each pulse channel has an internal duty step counter indexing into the selected waveform. The duty step increments at 8× the channel's frequency. Cannot be reset except by turning APU off. Retriggering resets the duty step timer; retriggering often enough prevents it from advancing.

First output of a newly started pulse channel is always digital zero.

## Wave Channel (CH3) Sample Index

CH3 has an internal sample index counter incrementing at 32× the channel's frequency. Each increment reads the corresponding nibble from wave RAM. Sample #0 is skipped on startup.

CH3 stores each read sample in a buffer and emits it continuously. Retriggering does NOT clear this buffer — last sample ever read repeats. Buffer IS cleared when APU turns on (emits digital 0).

Output level control shifts the DIGITAL value, not analog. Mid-playback changes bias toward 0 → analog biased toward 1; HPF smooths over time.

## Noise Channel (CH4) LFSR

CH4 uses a 16-bit LFSR: 15 bits state + 1 bit for next shift-in value.

On each tick:
1. XOR of bit 0 and bit 1 written to bit 15 (1 if identical, 0 otherwise)
2. In short mode (7-bit): bit 15 also copied to bit 7
3. LFSR shifted right; bit 0 selects between 0 and chosen volume

LFSR set to 0 on trigger.

**Lock-up**: If active portion of LFSR contains only 1s, only 1s are generated — CH4 never changes (silenced until retriggered). Can occur by switching from 15-bit to 7-bit mode when bottom 7 bits are all 1s.

text:
## Game Boy Advance Audio

The APU was heavily reworked for GBA:
- Mixing done digitally instead of by analog circuitry; sound converted to analog signal with offset added (see SOUNDBIAS in GBATEK).
- No physical DACs. Emulated digitally: a disabled DAC behaves like an enabled DAC receiving 0 as input.
- CH3's DAC output is INVERTED. Causes a loud spike when disabled — disconnect channel via NR51 before accessing wave RAM.

None of the additional GBA features (more wave RAM, digital FIFOs) are available to CGB programs.

text:
## Obscure Audio Behavior

- Volume envelope and sweep timers treat a period of 0 as 8.
- After power-on, first duty step of CH1/CH2 after initial trigger plays as if it were 0. Duty cycle clocking disabled until first trigger.
- Triggering CH3: first sample played is the previous one in high nibble of buffer; next sample is second nibble from wave table (first byte not loaded on trigger). First nibble not played until waveform loops.
- Triggering CH1/CH2: low two bits of frequency timer NOT modified.
- Extra length clocking: writing to NRx4 when DIV-APU next step doesn't clock the length timer. If length was previously disabled, now enabled, and timer non-zero → decremented. If decrement makes zero and trigger clear → channel disabled. CGB-02 differs (only needs prior disable); fixed on CGB-04/CGB-05. Breaks Prehistorik Man.
- Triggering when DIV-APU next step doesn't clock length timer, length now enabled, and length set to 64 (256 for CH3) because previously zero → set to 63 instead (255 for CH3).
- Triggering when DIV-APU next step will clock volume envelope: envelope timer reloaded with one greater than normal.
- Noise channel clock shift of 14 or 15 results in LFSR receiving no clocks.
- Clearing sweep direction bit in NR10 after at least one subtraction-mode sweep calculation since last trigger → CH1 immediately disabled. Prevents lowering then raising frequency without re-trigger.

text:
## Obscure Audio Behavior (continued)

**Wave RAM corruption on DMG**: Triggering CH3 while it reads a sample byte alters the first four bytes of wave RAM. If reading one of first 4 bytes: only first byte rewritten with read byte. If reading bytes 4-15: first FOUR bytes rewritten with aligned source bytes (e.g., reading byte 9 → bytes 8-11 copied to first 4). Avoid by writing 0 then $80 to NR30 before retriggering. Duck Tales encounters this issue.

**Zombie mode**: Volume can be manually altered mid-playback via NRx2 writes. CGB-02/CGB-04 most consistent:
- If old envelope period was zero and envelope still auto-updating: volume += 1; if decrease mode: volume += 2.
- Mode change (increase↔decrease): volume = 16 - volume.
- Only low 4 bits kept. DMG units have inconsistent behavior.
- Useful consistent pattern: write $V8 to NRx2 for initial volume V, then $08 to increment by 1 during playback (repeat 15 times to decrement by 1).

**HPF implementation**: When all DACs off, master volume disconnected, output = 0. Any DAC on connects HPF capacitor that removes DC component. Charge factor: 0.999958^(4194304/rate). DMG uses 0.999958; MGB&CGB use 0.998943.

text:
# CPU Instruction Set

The Game Boy's SM83 processor has a CISC, variable-length instruction set. The first byte of each instruction is the opcode. Instructions performing identical operations with different parameters can be grouped together.

Placeholders and their values:
| Placeholder | Encoding |
|---|---|
| r8 | 0:b, 1:c, 2:d, 3:e, 4:h, 5:l, 6:[hl], 7:a |
| r16 | 0:bc, 1:de, 2:hl, 3:sp |
| r16stk | 0:bc, 1:de, 2:hl, 3:af |
| r16mem | 0:bc, 1:de, 2:hl+, 3:hl- |
| cond | 0:nz, 1:z, 2:nc, 3:c |
| b3 | 0-7: A 3-bit bit index |
| tgt3 | 0-7: rst's target address / 8 |
| imm8 | The following byte (instruction +1 extra byte) |
| imm16 | Following two bytes, little-endian (instruction +2 extra bytes) |

Note: [hl+] and [hl-] can also be notated [hli] and [hld].

Instruction families are grouped into four blocks by the first two bits of the opcode.

text: ## Block 1: 8-bit register-to-register loads ```text ld r8, r8 7:"0" 6:"1" 5-3:"Dest (r8)" 2-0:"Source (r8)" ``` **Exception**: trying to encode `ld [hl], [hl]` instead yields the `halt` instruction: ```text halt 7:"0" 6:"1" 5:"1" 4:"1" 3:"0" 2:"1" 1:"1" 0:"0" ``` ## Block 2: 8-bit arithmetic ```text add a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"0" 4:"0" 3:"0" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" adc a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"0" 4:"0" 3:"1" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" sub a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"0" 4:"1" 3:"0" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" sbc a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"0" 4:"1" 3:"1" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" and a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"1" 4:"0" 3:"0" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" xor a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"1" 4:"0" 3:"1" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" or a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"1" 4:"1" 3:"0" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" cp a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"1" 4:"1" 3:"1" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" ``` ## Block 3 Block 3 includes immediate arithmetic (`add a, imm8`, `adc a, imm8`, `sub a, imm8`, `sbc a, imm8`, `and a, imm8`, `xor a, imm8`, `or a, imm8`, `cp a, imm8`), control flow (`ret cond`, `ret`, `reti`, `jp cond, imm16`, `jp imm16`, `jp hl`, `call cond, imm16`, `call imm16`, `rst tgt3`), stack operations (`pop r16stk`, `push r16stk`), the prefix instruction, I/O loads (`ldh [c], a`, `ldh [imm8], a`, `ld [imm16], a`, `ldh a, [c]`, `ldh a, [imm8]`, `ld a, [imm16]`), SP operations (`add sp, imm8`, `ld hl, sp + imm8`, `ld sp, hl`), and `di`/`ei`. The following opcodes are **invalid**, and hard-lock the CPU until the console is powered off: $D3, $DB, $DD, $E3, $E4, $EB, $EC, $ED, $F4, $FC, and $FD.

text:
# CPU Registers and Flags

## Registers
| 16-bit | Hi | Lo | Name/Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| AF | A | - | Accumulator & Flags |
| BC | B | C | BC |
| DE | D | E | DE |
| HL | H | L | HL |
| SP | - | - | Stack Pointer |
| PC | - | - | Program Counter/Pointer |

Most registers can be accessed as one 16-bit register or two separate 8-bit registers.

## The Flags Register (lower 8 bits of AF)
| Bit | Name | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | Z | Zero flag — set iff result is zero; used by conditional jumps |
| 6 | N | Subtraction flag (BCD) — indicates previous instruction was subtraction |
| 5 | H | Half Carry flag (BCD) — carry for lower 4 bits of result |
| 4 | C | Carry flag — set on overflow/underflow; used by conditional jumps, ADC, SBC, RL, RLA etc. |

Bits 3-0 are unused.

## The Carry Flag Details
Set when:
- 8-bit addition result > $FF
- 16-bit addition result > $FFFF
- Subtraction/comparison result < 0 (like Z80/x86, unlike 65XX/ARM)
- Rotate/shift operation shifts out a "1" bit

text:
## The BCD Flags (N, H)

Used by DAA instruction only. N indicates whether the previous instruction was a subtraction; H indicates carry for lower 4 bits of result.
DAA also uses C flag, which must indicate carry for upper 4 bits.
After adding/subtracting two BCD numbers, DAA converts the result to BCD format.
BCD numbers range from $00-$99 rather than $00-$FF.

Because only two flags (C and H) exist for carry-outs of BCD digits:
- DAA is ineffective for 16-bit operations (4 digits)
- Use with INC/DEC has limits (they don't affect C-flag)
