text:
# CPU Comparison with Z80

## Comparison with 8080

The Game Boy CPU has more in common with Intel 8080 than Zilog Z80. Missing some 8080 instructions but supports JR and almost all CB-prefixed instructions. All known assemblers use Z80-style syntax.

Unlike 8080/Z80: no dedicated I/O bus, no IN/OUT opcodes. I/O ports accessed via normal LD or new LD ($FF00+n) opcodes.

Removed from 8080: sign flag, parity/overflow flag, and all 12 RET/CALL/JP instructions conditioned on them. Also removed: EX (SP),HL (XTHL) and EX DE,HL (XCHG).

## Comparison with Z80

In addition to removed 8080 instructions:
- All exchange instructions removed (including total absence of second register set)
- All DD- and FD-prefixed instructions missing (no IX/IY registers)
- All ED-prefixed instructions missing (16-bit memory accesses mostly gone, 16-bit arithmetic heavily cut down)
- IN/OUT (C) replaced with LD ($FF00+C)
- Block instructions gone; autoincrementing HL accesses added
- Speed: ~4 MHz Z80 equivalent (8 MHz in CGB double speed); all instruction times rounded to multiple of 4 cycles

text:
# Timer Obscure Behaviour

DIV is the visible part of the system counter, which constantly increments every M-cycle unless CPU is in STOP mode.

## Relation between Timer and Divider Register

The bits of the system counter connect to a multiplexer then to a falling-edge detector. This causes odd behaviors:

- Writing to DIV (resetting system counter) can reset the bit currently selected by TAC's multiplexer, sending an early "Timer tick" and/or "DIV-APU event" pulse.
- Changing TAC's Clock select from a set bit to an unset bit sends a "Timer tick" pulse. Example: if system counter = $3FF0 and TAC = $FC, writing $05 or $06 triggers a tick; $04 or $07 won't.
- On monochrome consoles (DMG), disabling the timer when the selected bit is set sends one "Timer tick". Does NOT happen on CGB.
- On CGB, a TAC write that meets the above conditions AND turns the timer on may or may not send a tick; behavior varies between individual consoles.

text:
## Timer Overflow Behavior

When TIMA overflows, TMA's value is copied to TIMA and the timer flag is set in IF — but one M-cycle later. TIMA equals $00 for the M-cycle after overflow.
Only happens from incrementing; cannot be triggered by manually writing to TIMA.

Unexpected behaviors:
1. Writing to TIMA during cycle A (overflow cycle) acts as if overflow didn't happen! TMA not copied, IF bit 2 not set. Writing to DIV/TAC/other registers won't prevent IF flag or TIMA reload.
2. Writing to TIMA during cycle B will be ignored; TIMA = TMA at end of cycle anyway (TAL cells constantly copy input).
3. Writing to TMA during cycle B copies the new value to TIMA as well, on same cycle.

Technical explanation: Writing to TIMA blocks falling edge detection via AND gate. The "Load" signal stays enabled for cycle B; TAL cells constantly copy input. Write to TIMA goes through but is overwritten by multiplexer emitting TMA's value.

text:
# halt

`halt` pauses the CPU (reducing power consumption). CPU wakes when an interrupt is pending: `[IE] & [IF] != 0`.

With IME set: CPU wakes, interrupt handler called normally before next instruction.

With IME not set:
- No interrupt pending: halt executes normally; resumes on next interrupt (not handled since IME=0)
- Interrupt already pending: halt exits immediately, triggering the **halt bug**

## Halt Bug

When executed with IME=0 and `[IE] & [IF] != 0`, halt ends but PC fails to increment normally.

Under most circumstances: byte after halt is read again (repeats if that byte is another halt).

Special cases:
- **halt preceded by ei**: ei's effect delayed one instruction, so IME still zero. Interrupt serviced and handler called, returns to halt which waits for next interrupt.
- **halt followed by rst**: rst's return address points at the rst itself (not byte after it). A ret would execute the rst again.

If bugged halt is preceded by ei AND followed by rst: the ei case "wins".

text:
# Power-Up Sequence

CPU starts at $0000, not $0100. A boot ROM burned into the CPU is mapped over cartridge ROM initially, responsible for boot-up animation before handing control to game ROM at $0100.

Known boot ROMs:
| Name | Size | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| DMG0 | 256 | Blinks on failed checks, no ® |
| DMG | 256 | Standard monochrome |
| MGB | 256 | One-byte difference to DMG (writes $FF not $01) |
| SGB | 256 | Forwards logo to SGB BIOS, no checks |
| SGB2 | 256 | Same diff to SGB as MGB has to DMG |
| CGB0 | 256+1792 | Does not init wave RAM |
| CGB | 256+1792 | Split in two parts, header in middle |
| AGB0 | 256+1792 | Increments B register for GBA identification |
| AGB | 256+1792 | Fixes "logo TOCTTOU" |

## Monochrome Models (DMG0, DMG, MGB)
Boot ROM reads logo from header, unpacks to VRAM, scrolls it down. No cartridge = black box ($FF reads). After scrolling: plays "ba-ding!", reads logo again comparing to stored copy, checks header checksum. If either fails → boot ROM locks up.

Finally writes to BANK register at $FF50 (unmaps boot ROM). `ldh [$FF50], a` at $00FE is two bytes; first cartridge instruction executes at $0100. A register value passed: DMG writes $01, MGB writes $FF.

DMG0 variant: performs checks BEFORE display; on failure screen blinks white/black alternately. Lacks ® symbol.

text:
## Super Game Boy (SGB, SGB2)
Boot ROMs do NOT perform header checks. Set up logo in VRAM from header, then send entire header to SGB BIOS via packet transfer using bytes $F1, $F3, $F5, $F7, $F9, $FB. Unmaps itself without checks; SGB BIOS on SNES verifies logo and checksum instead.

Packet-sending routine: transferring a set bit takes one cycle less than reset bit; total time depends on cartridge header contents. Waits 4 VBlanks after each packet.

## Color Models (CGB0, CGB, AGB0, AGB)
Boot ROM is 2048 bytes, split in two parts: $0000-00FF and $0200-08FF (to access cartridge header at $0100-014F).

Unpacks logo to VRAM + copies to HRAM buffer. Reads/decompresses again for smaller "GAME BOY" logo below big one. Sets up compatibility palettes, plays animation.

During animation: if CGB compatibility byte bit 7 reset (monochrome game), user can pick palette override; each choice delays end by 30 frames.

Checks logo from HRAM buffer (only first half checked despite full copy). Fades BG palettes to white, sets hardware to compatibility mode. Writes $11 to unmap.

CGB0: does NOT init wave RAM ($FF30-$FF3F); causes different title screen music in R-Type.

text:
## Compatibility Palettes
Boot ROM auto-colorizes monochrome games on GBC. In DMG compatibility mode, CGB palettes still used: BG uses palette 0 (attribute map all zeros), OBJ uses OBP0/OBP1 depending on bit 4 of attributes. BGP/OBP0/OBP1 index into CGB palettes instead of DMG shades.

Palette ID algorithm:
1. Check old licensee code ($33 → check new licensee = "01"; otherwise check old = $01). Failure → palette ID $00.
2. Sum all 16 game title bytes as "title checksum"
3. Find checksum in table, record index. Not found → $00. Index ≤ 64 → use as-is. Otherwise correct by fourth letter of title (search another table; row × 14 added to index).

Resulting ID picks 3 palettes from a complex mechanism. User can override with button combos during animation.

If ID is $43 or $58: Nintendo logo tilemap written to VRAM (for games animating the logo).

## Logo Check and Bypass
Logo check deters piracy via trademark law (*Sega v. Accolade* ruled it's okay if necessary for console operation).

Bypass methods:
- TOCTTOU: exploit timing between logo read for display vs verification (patched on later AGB; requires custom hardware)
- CGB/AGB only: boot ROM copies full logo to HRAM but checks only first half. Top-half-correct/bottom-half-wrong logo passes.

text:
# CGB Registers

This chapter covers Game Boy Color registers not in normal categories. Most CGB registers are in the Video Display chapter.

## Unlocking CGB Functions
Byte $0143 in cartridge header must be changed: $80 for dual CGB/monochrome support, $C0 for CGB-only. Otherwise CGB operates in monochrome "Non CGB" compatibility mode.

## Detecting CGB (and GBA)
A register = $11 after startup indicates CGB or GBA. When A=$11, examine B-register bit 0: cleared = CGB, set = GBA (for repaired color palette data matching).

## LCD VRAM DMA Transfers (HDMA)
### $FF51-$FF52 — HDMA1/HDMA2: Source address [write-only]
Specifies transfer source. Should be ROM/SRAM/WRAM ($0000-7FF0 or $A000-DFF0). Lower 4 bits ignored (treated as 0). VRAM source causes garbage.

### $FF53-$FF54 — HDMA3/HDMA4: Destination address [write-only]
Specifies destination in $8000-9FF0. Only bits 12-4 respected; lower 4 and upper 3 ignored (always VRAM).

### $FF55 — HDMA5: Length/mode/start
Writing starts transfer. Lower 7 bits = length ($10-$800 bytes, values $00-$7F). Bit 7 = mode:
- **Bit 7=0 (General-Purpose DMA)**: All data at once; program halted until complete. Blindly copies even if LCD accessing VRAM — use only when display off, during VBlank, or short HBlank blocks. After completion FF55 = $FF.
- **Bit 7=1 (HBlank DMA)**: Transfers $10 bytes per HBlank (LY=0-143). No transfer during VBlank (LY=144-153), resumes at LY=00. CPU halted during transfers but runs between blocks. Don't change destination VRAM bank or source ROM/RAM bank until complete.

Reading FF55 returns remaining length ($FF = complete). Terminate HBlank by writing 0 to bit 7; lower 7 bits show remaining blocks (minus 1), bit 7 reads as 1. Stopping doesn't set HDMA1-4 to $FF.

**Warning**: Don't start HBlank DMA during HBlank period (STAT mode 0). Destination overflow stops transfer prematurely.

text:
## HDMA Transfer Timings
Both Normal and Double Speed: ~8 μs per $10 byte block. 8 M-cycles normal speed, 16 fast M-cycles double speed. Always 2 bytes/μs transferred.
Older MBC controllers (MBC1-3) and slower ROMs not guaranteed to support General-Purpose or HBlank DMA.
VBlank allows ~2280 bytes transfer = up to 142.5 tiles.

## VRAM Banks
CGB has twice DMG's VRAM, banked with different purposes per bank.

### $FF4F — VBK: VRAM Bank
Only bit 0 matters; all other bits ignored. Reading returns current bank in bit 0, other bits set to 1.

**VRAM Bank 1**: Split like bank 0. $8000-97FF stores tiles (accessible same time as bank 0). $9800-9FFF contains attributes for corresponding Tile Maps.

## $FF4D — KEY1/SPD: Prepare Speed Switch
Bit layout:
- Bit 7 (read-only): Current speed. 0 = Normal, 1 = Double-speed.
- Bit 0 (R/W): Switch armed. 0 = No, 1 = Armed.

Speed switch procedure:
```
IF KEY1_BIT7 != DESIRED_SPEED THEN
   IE = $00; JOYP = $30; KEY1 = $01; STOP
ENDIF
```
CGB starts in Normal Speed. Double speed increases power consumption.

In Double Speed Mode, twice as fast: CPU (2.10 MHz, 1 M-cycle ≈ 0.5 μs), Timer/Divider Registers, Serial Port, OAM DMA.
Unchanged: LCD Video Controller, HDMA Transfer to VRAM, all Sound Timings/Frequencies.

After `stop`: CPU stops for 2050 M-cycles (8200 T-cycles). DIV doesn't tick; some audio events not processed. VRAM/OAM locking frozen — behavior depends on PPU mode at start: Mode 0/1 = no video memory access, black pixels; Mode 2 = VRAM accessible but not OAM (BG renders, sprites don't); Mode 3 = everything works correctly.

text:
## $FF56 — RP: Infrared Communications Port (CGB only)
Input/output data through CGB's built-in IR port. For reading, bits 6-7 must be set ($C0+); bit 0 cleared to avoid receiving own signal. Reset to $00 after use to reduce power.

Bit layout:
- Bits 7-6: Read enable. 0 = Disable (bit 1 reads 1), 3 = Enable.
- Bit 1 (read-only): Receiving. 0 = IR signal received, 1 = Normal.
- Bit 0: Emitting. 0 = LED off, 1 = LED on.

Receiver adapts to ambient IR level; permanent ON treated as normal after a while. GBA does not include IR port.

## $FF4C — KEY0/SYS: CPU Mode Select (CGB only)
Written only by CGB boot ROM; locked after BANK register write unmapps boot ROM. Cannot be changed without reset.
- Bit 2: DMG compatibility mode. 0 = Disabled (full CGB), 1 = Enabled (DMG-only cartridges).

**PGB Mode**: Setting bit 3 may relate to external LCD control. Not well researched.

## $FF6C — OPRI: Object Priority Mode (CGB only)
Flag for object priority mode. DMG prioritizes by x-coordinate; CGB by OAM location. Set by boot ROM after checking CGB compatibility.
- Bit 0: Priority mode. 0 = CGB-style, 1 = DMG-style.

Effect if PGB value written to KEY0 before STOP executed (instant effect). No instant effect in CGB/DMG mode after boot ROM unmapped.

## $FF70 — SVBK/WBK: WRAM Bank (CGB only)
CGB has 32 KiB internal RAM in 8 banks of 4 KiB. Bank 0 always at $C000-CFFF; banks 1-7 selectable into $D000-DFFF.
- Bits 2-0: WRAM bank. Writing value maps corresponding bank to $D000-DFFF, except 0 which maps bank 1.

## Undocumented Registers
$FF72-$FF73: Fully R/W, initial $00 (CGB only).
$FF74: Fully R/W in CGB mode ($00); read-only locked at $FF otherwise.
$FF75: Only bits 4-6 R/W, initial 0 (CGB only).

text:
# MBCs (Memory Bank Controllers)

The Game Boy's 16-bit address bus offers limited space for ROM and RAM. MBC chips in the cartridge expand available address space via bank switching.

Required/preferred MBC type specified at byte $0147 of ROM (cartridge header).

## MBC Timing Issues
Only MBC5 is guaranteed by Nintendo to support CGB Double Speed Mode's tighter timing. Older MBCs (MBC1-3) may not be fast enough, though self-made MBC1-EPROM cards appear stable in Double Speed. Possible workaround: use Double Speed only during periods using internal RAM code/data.

## MBC Unmapped RAM Bank Access
If an unmapped RAM bank is selected (out of bounds), most MBCs wrap around to a valid address:
`((address - external_ram_start) + (active_ram_bank * ram_bank_size)) % max_external_ram_size`

Games ≤ 32 KiB ROM don't require an MBC chip. ROM directly mapped at $0000-7FFF.
Optionally up to 8 KiB RAM connectable at $A000-BFFF using a discrete logic decoder instead of full MBC chip.

text:
# MBC1
Max 2 MiB ROM and/or 32 KiB RAM. First MBC chip; newer chips work similarly.

Default: up to 512 KiB ROM with 32 KiB banked RAM. Some carts wire the 2-bit RAM banking register as ROM banking extension for up to 2 MiB ROM (fixed 8 KiB RAM). All MBC1 carts ≥ 1 MiB use this alternate wiring.

Memory range $0000-7FFF used for both reading ROM and writing MBC control registers.

## Memory Regions
**$0000-3FFF**: First 16 KiB (bank 00). In 1 MiB+ carts, mode 1 allows second banking register to apply here too (banks $20/$40/$60 for large ROM, or $10/$20/$30 for MBC1M).

**$4000-7FFF**: Any of further 16 KiB banks. If main 5-bit register = 0, reads as bank 1. For 1 MiB+ carts: banks accessible via $0000-3FFF region not accessible here; maps to +1 bank ($21/$41/$61 or $11/$21/$31).

**$A000-BFFF**: External RAM if any. Only accessible when RAM enabled; otherwise reads return open bus (often $FF, not guaranteed), writes ignored. 8 KiB or 32 KiB (four 8K banks). 32 KiB only in carts ≤ 512 KiB ROM.

## Registers (all default to $00 on power-up; ROM bank treated as $01)
**$0000-1FFF — RAM Enable [write-only]**: Write value with lower nibble = $A to enable. Any other value disables. Recommend disabling after access to protect contents.

**$2000-3FFF — ROM Bank Number [write-only]**: 5-bit register ($01-$1F) for $4000-7FFF region. Higher bits discarded. $00 → $01 (can't duplicate bank 0). Over-range values masked to cart size. For ≤256 KiB carts, setting bit 5 prevents 00→01 translation while selecting bank 0.

**$4000-5FFF — RAM Bank / Upper ROM Bits [write-only]**: 2-bit register for RAM bank ($00-$03, 32 KiB carts) or upper bits (bits 5-6 of ROM number, 1 MiB+ carts). If neither large enough, does nothing.

**$6000-7FFF — Banking Mode Select [write-only]**: Bit 0 selects mode. 0 = simple (default): $0000-3FFF and $A000-BFFF locked to bank 0. 1 = advanced: both regions can be bank-switched via $4000-5FFF register.

Formula for large carts: `Selected ROM Bank = (Secondary Bank << 5) + ROM Bank`

text:
## MBC1M: 1 MiB Multi-Game Compilation Carts
Alternative wiring ignores top bit of main ROM banking register (effectively 4-bit, though full 5-bit still used for 00→01 translation). 2-bit register applied to bits 4-5 of bank number (not usual bits 5-6).
In mode 1: 2-bit register selects banks $00, $10, $20, or $30 (vs. usual $00, $20, $40, $60). Uses mode 1 remapping of $0000-3FFF to switch games; each game thinks it's running from a 256 KiB cart.

Identification: Nintendo copyright header in bank $10. Bad dumps have duplicate content in banks $10-$1F (dupe of $00-$0F) and $30-$3F (dupe of $20-$2F). Known bad dump: Mortal Kombat I & II.

Conversion to regular MBC1: Increase ROM size to 2 MiB, duplicate each sub-ROM ($00-$0F → $10-$1F, original $10-$1F → $20-$2F duplicated into $30-$3F).

### Addressing (MBC1M)
**$0000-3FFF**: Mode 0 = bank 0. Mode 1 = from $4000-5FFF register at bits 18-19.
**$4000-7FFF**: Both modes = $4000-5FFF at bits 18-19, $2000-3FFF (bit 4 unused) at bits 16-13.

text:
# MBC2
Max 256 KiB ROM and 512×4 bits RAM.

## Memory Regions
**$0000-3FFF**: First 16 KiB of ROM (bank 0).

**$4000-7FFF**: ROM banks $01-$0F. Same as MBC1 but only 16 total banks.

**$A000-A1FF**: Built-in RAM — 512 half-bytes (built into MBC2 chip). Requires external battery for save. Only lower 4 bits used; upper 4 bits undefined, don't rely on them.

**$A200-BFFF**: 15 echoes of $A000-A1FF. Only bottom 9 address bits index internal RAM (access repeats). Same 4-bit limitation as $A000-A1FF.

## Registers
### $0000-3FFF — RAM Enable + ROM Bank Number [write-only]
Single address range controls both RAM enable and ROM bank. Bit 8 of address determines which:

**Bit 8 clear (even upper byte)**: Controls RAM enable. Lower 4 bits = $A → enabled; any other value → disabled. Addresses: $0000-00FF, $0200-02FF, ..., $3E00-3EFF. Default: disabled.

**Bit 8 set (odd upper byte)**: Controls ROM bank at $4000-7FFF. Lower 4 bits = bank number. Bank 0 → bank 1. Addresses: $0100-01FF, $0300-03FF, ..., $3F00-3FFF. Default: bank 1.

text:
# MBC3
Max 2 MiB ROM and/or 32 KiB RAM plus built-in RTC. RTC requires external 32.768 kHz quartz oscillator and battery.

## Memory Regions
**$0000-3FFF**: First 16 KiB (bank 00).
**$4000-7FFF**: Banks $01-$7F. Same as MBC1 except banks $20, $40, $60 are now accessible.
**$A000-BFFF**: External RAM bank or RTC register depending on selection.

## Registers
**$0000-1FFF — RAM and Timer Enable [write-only]**: Same as MBC1. $0A enables RAM AND RTC registers; $00 disables both.

**$2000-3FFF — ROM Bank Number [write-only]**: Full 7 bits written directly ($01-$7F). $00 → bank $01 (same as MBC1).

**$4000-5FFF — RAM Bank / RTC Register Select [write-only]**:
| Value | Selection |
|---|---|
| $00-$07 | Corresponding RAM bank |
| $08-$0C | Corresponding RTC register |

**$6000-7FFF — Latch Clock Data [write-only]**: Write $00 then $01 to latch current time into RTC registers. Latched data won't change until latched again (allows reading while clock ticks).

## RTC Registers
| Register | Name | Range |
|---|---|---|
| $08 | Seconds | 0-59 ($00-$3B) |
| $09 | Minutes | 0-59 ($00-$3B) |
| $0A | Hours | 0-23 ($00-$17) |
| $0B | Day Counter (lower 8 bits) | $00-$FF |
| $0C | Day Counter upper bit + flags | Bit 0: Day counter MSB; Bit 6: Halt (0=Active, 1=Stop); Bit 7: Carry overflow |

Day counter: 9 bits total, range 0-511 ($000-$1FF). Carry bit set on overflow; remains until reset. Store offset in battery RAM to count beyond 511 days (add counter to offset each read, reset counter).

**Delays**: Wait 4 μs (4 M-cycles normal speed) between separate RTC register accesses.

text:
# MBC5
Maps up to 8 MiB (64 Mbit) ROM. First MBC guaranteed for CGB Double Speed mode.

## Memory Regions
**$0000-3FFF**: First 16 KiB (bank 00).
**$4000-7FFF**: Banks $00-$1FF. Unlike other MBCs, bank 0 is actually bank 0 (not remapped to 1). Up to 512 banks.
**$A000-BFFF**: RAM banks $00-$0F if any. Sizes: 8 KiB, 32 KiB, or 128 KiB.

## Registers
**$0000-1FFF — RAM Enable [write-only]**: Same as MBC1. $0A enables; $00 disables. Actual MBCs enable when bottom 4 bits = $A ($0A, $1A, etc.), but relying on this is not recommended.

**$2000-2FFF — ROM Bank Lower Bits [write-only]**: 8 least significant bits of bank number. Writing 0 gives actual bank 0 (unlike other MBCs).

**$3000-3FFF — ROM Bank Upper Bit [write-only]**: 9th bit of bank number.

**$4000-5FFF — RAM Bank Number [write-only]**: $00-$0F maps corresponding external RAM bank to $A000-BFFF.

## Rumble
On cartridges with rumble motor, bit 3 of RAM bank register connects to rumble circuitry instead of RAM chip. Setting bit 3 = 1 enables rumble; reset to disable. Control intensity by turning on/off repeatedly (see Pokémon Pinball examples).

text:
# The Cartridge Header
Header at $0100-$014F provides game info and expected hardware.

## $0100-0103 — Entry Point
Boot ROM jumps to $0100 after displaying logo. Most games: `nop` + `jp $0150` (4 bytes).

## $0104-0133 — Nintendo Logo
48-byte bitmap displayed on power-on. Must match exact dump or boot ROM locks up:
```
CE ED 66 66 CC 0D 00 0B 03 73 00 83 00 0C 00 0D
00 08 11 1F 88 89 00 0E DC CC 6E E6 DD DD D9 99
BB BB 67 63 6E 0E EC CC DD DC 99 9F BB B9 33 3E
```
Bytes $0104-$011B = top half, $011C-$0133 = bottom half. Each nibble encodes 4 pixels (MSB=leftmost). Groups laid out top-to-bottom, left-to-right. Monochrome models upscale by factor of 2.
CGB+ only checks top half ($18 bytes).

## $0134-0143 — Title
Upper-case ASCII game title. If < 16 chars, pad with $00. On later carts, parts overlap with other fields (reducing actual title to 15 or 11 chars).

## $013F-0142 — Manufacturer Code
Newer cartridges: 4-character manufacturer code (uppercase ASCII). Purpose unknown.

## $0143 — CGB Flag
Determines Color mode vs monochrome compatibility:
| Value | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $80 | Supports CGB enhancements, backwards compatible with DMG |
| $C0 | CGB only (bit 6 ignored by hardware; same as $80) |
Setting bit 7 triggers write to KEY0 register for CPU mode selection.

text:
## $0144-0145 — New Licensee Code
Two-character ASCII publisher code. Only meaningful if old licensee ($014B) = $33 (post-SGB games).
Notable codes: 01=Nintendo R&D1, 08=Capcom, 13=EA, 18/38=Hudson Soft, 31=Nintendo, 32=Bandai, 34/54=Konami, 78=THQ.

## $0146 — SGB Flag
$03 = game supports SGB functions. Any other value (typically $00) causes SGB to ignore command packets.

## $0147 — Cartridge Type
Indicates mapper and hardware:
| Code | Type |
|---|---|
| $00 | ROM ONLY |
| $01-$03 | MBC1 / +RAM / +RAM+BATTERY |
| $05-$06 | MBC2 / +BATTERY |
| $0F-$13 | MBC3 variants (TIMER, RAM, BATTERY) |
| $19-$1E | MBC5 variants (RAM, RUMBLE, BATTERY) |
| $20 | MBC6 |
| $22 | MBC7+SENSOR+RUMBLE+RAM+BATTERY |
| $FC-$FF | POCKET CAMERA, BANDAI TAMA5, HuC3, HuC1 |

## $0148 — ROM Size
`32 KiB × (1 << value)`: $00=32KiB(2 banks), $01=64KiB(4), ..., $08=8MiB(512). Unofficial sizes: $52=1.1MiB, $53=1.2MiB, $54=1.5MiB (no known cartridges use these).

## $0149 — RAM Size
$00=None, $02=8KiB(1 bank), $03=32KiB(4 banks), $04=128KiB(16 banks), $05=64KiB(8 banks). MBC2's built-in 512×4 bits not counted here.

## $014A — Destination Code
$00 = Japan (and possibly overseas); $01 = Overseas only.

text:
## $014B — Old Licensee Code
Pre-SGB publisher byte. Value $33 means use new licensee codes ($0144-0145) instead.
Notable old codes: 01=Nintendo, 08=Capcom, 13=EA, 18=Hudson Soft, 31=Nintendo, 32=Bandai, 34=Konami, 78=THQ. Full table has ~150 known codes.

## $014C — Mask ROM Version Number
Usually $00.

## $014D — Header Checksum
8-bit checksum of bytes $0134-$014C:
```c
uint8_t checksum = 0;
for (address = 0x0134; address <= 0x014C; address++)
    checksum = checksum - rom[address] - 1;
```
Boot ROM verifies this. Mismatch → boot ROM locks up, cartridge won't run.

## $014E-014F — Global Checksum
16-bit big-endian sum of all ROM bytes (excluding these two checksum bytes). Not verified by boot ROM; only checked by Pokémon Stadium's "GB Tower" emulator.

text:
## Game Genie (ROM Patches)

Game Shark and Game Genie are external cartridge adapters plugged between the Game Boy and the actual game cartridge.

Game Genie codes consist of nine-digit hex numbers, formatted as `ABC-DEF-GHI`. Digit meaning:
- `AB`: New data to write
- `FCDE`: Memory address, XORed by $F000
- `GI`: Old data, XORed by $BA and rotated left by two
- `H`: Unknown — possibly checksum and/or other purpose

The address should be in ROM area $0000-$7FFF. The adapter permanently compares address/old data with the address/data being read by the game, replacing data with new data if necessary. This method (more or less) prohibits unwanted patching of wrong memory banks. It may also be possible to patch external RAM. Newer devices reportedly allow specifying only the first six digits optionally.

Three codes can be used at once.

text:
## Game Shark (RAM Patches)

Game Shark codes consist of eight hexadecimal digits:
| Bits | Field |
|---|---|
| 0-1 | SRAM bank |
| 2-3 | New value |
| 4-7 | Address |

Example: cheat code `010238CD` switches to SRAM bank $01, and writes $02 at address $CD38.

Patching is implemented by hooking the original VBlank interrupt handler, re-writing RAM values each frame. This steals some CPU time and cannot be used to patch program code in ROM (only RAM). 10-25 codes can be used simultaneously.

text:
# MBC6

MBC6 is an unusual MBC containing two separately switchable ROM banks ($4000 and $6000) and RAM banks ($A000 and $B000), SRAM, and an 8 Mbit Macronix MX29F008TC-14 flash memory chip. Only used in one game: Net de Get: Minigame @ 100, which uses the Mobile Adapter to connect to the web to download mini-games onto local flash.

Both ROM banks and both RAM banks are views into the same ROM and RAM but with separately adjustable offsets. Since banked regions are smaller, effective number of banks is twice normal: 8 KiB ROM banks instead of 16 KiB, 4 KiB RAM banks instead of 8 KiB.

## Memory Regions
| Range | Description |
|---|---|
| $0000-3FFF | ROM Bank 00 (Read Only) — first 16 KiB of ROM |
| $4000-5FFF | ROM/Flash Bank A, banks 00-7F (R/W for flash, RO for ROM), switchable independently of Bank B |
| $6000-7FFF | ROM/Flash Bank B, banks 00-7F (R/W for flash, RO for ROM), switchable independently of Bank A |
| $A000-AFFF | RAM Bank A, banks 00-07 (Read/Write), switchable independently of Bank B |
| $B000-BFFF | RAM Bank B, banks 00-07 (Read/Write), switchable independently of Bank A |

text:
## Flash Commands

The Macronix MX29F008TC-14 flash chip is mapped directly into Bank A or B address space. Standard flash access commands apply.

Command sequence: write $AA to $Y555, then $55 to $XAAA (where X=4/6 and Y=5/7 depending on bank region), followed by command-specific bytes.

| Sequence | Command |
|---|---|
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $80→$Y555, $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $30→addr | Erase sector (128 KiB region to $FF) |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $80→$Y555, $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $10→$Y555 | Erase chip (entire 1 MiB to $FF) |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $60→$Y555, $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $04→$Y555 | Erase hidden region* (256 bytes to $FF) |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $90→$Y555 | ID mode (JEDEC ID C2,81 at $XXX0,$XXX1) |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $77→$Y555, $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $77→$Y555 | Read hidden region |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $A0→$Y555 | Program mode |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $60→$Y555, $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $E0→$Y555 | Program hidden region* |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $60→$Y555, $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $40→$Y555 | Unprotect sector 0* |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $60→$Y555, $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $20→$Y555 | Protect sector 0* |
| $F0→any | Exit any command mode |

Commands marked * require Flash Write Enable bit = 1.

## Programming Procedure
Erase commands cause flash to read status bytes instead of values. Status bit 7 ($80) = operation finished → exit with $F0. Bit 4 ($10) = timeout. Bit 1 ($02) = sector 0 protection enabled.

Programming: erase sector first, activate program mode, write 128 aligned bytes, re-write final address (not $F0) to commit, wait for status completion, write $F0 to exit. If sector not erased first, programming may only AND bytes or fail entirely. Only way to set bits back to 1 is erase entire sector.

Erase sector: last byte ($30) written to address within target sector. Eight sectors of 128 KiB each. Bank = sector × 16 (e.g., sector 2 → bank 32 at $2000, write $30 to $4000-$5FFF).

Erase chip erases all 1 MiB but NOT hidden region. If sector 0 protected, only sectors 1-7 erased.

Hidden region: 256 bytes accessible via read hidden region command. Program in two 128-byte chunks using program hidden region command twice.

text:
# MBC7

MBC7 (Memory Bank Controller 7) is an MBC containing a 2-axis accelerometer (ADXL202E) and a 256 byte EEPROM (93LC56). $A000-BFFF does not directly address the EEPROM, as most MBCs do, but rather contains several registers that can be read or written one at a time. This makes EEPROM access very slow due to needing multiple writes per address.

## Memory

### $0000-3FFF — ROM Bank 00 (Read Only)
Contains the first 16 KiB of the ROM.

### $4000-7FFF — ROM Bank 00-7F (Read Only)
Same as for MBC5. (Bank 0 mapping needs confirmation).

text:
## Registers

### $A000-AFFF — RAM Registers (Read/Write)
Must be enabled via $0000 and $4000 region writes, otherwise reads return $FF and writes do nothing. Registers are addressed through bits 4-7 of the address. Bits 0-3 and 8-11 are ignored.

Accelerometer data must be latched before reading. Data is 16-bit and centered at the value $81D0. Earth's gravity affects the value by roughly $70, with larger acceleration providing a larger range. Maximum range is unknown.

text:
### Ax0x/Ax1x — Latch Accelerometer (Write Only)

Write $55 to Ax0x to erase the latched data (reset back to $8000), then $AA to Ax1x to latch the accelerometer and update the addressable registers. Reads return $FF. Other writes do not appear to do anything (partially unconfirmed).

You cannot re-latch the accelerometer value without first erasing it; attempts to do so yield no change.

Once the latch for a sample update has been triggered, the CPU should not be put into HALT for at least 1.2 milliseconds since HALTing turns off the PHI cartridge signal (the System Clock) which the MBC7 accelerometer sensor relies on. If that clock is turned off too soon, the X and Y values will have significantly more noise in the sampled data. The 1.2 ms duration is the same regardless of whether or not the CPU is in CGB Double-speed mode.

text:
### Ax2x/Ax3x — Accelerometer X value (Read Only)
Ax2x contains the low byte of the X value (lower values are towards the right and higher values are towards the left), and Ax3x contains the high byte. Reads $8000 before first latching.

### Ax4x/Ax5x — Accelerometer Y value (Read Only)
Ax4x contains the low byte of the Y value (lower values are towards the bottom and higher values are towards the top), and Ax5x contains the high byte. Reads $8000 before first latching.

### Ax6x/Ax7x — Unknown
Ax6x always reads $00 and Ax7x always reads $FF. Possibly reserved for Z axis, which does not exist on this accelerometer.

### Ax9x-AxFx — Unused
Reads out $FF.

### $B000-BFFF — Unknown
Only seems to read out $FF.

text:
### Ax8x — EEPROM (Read/Write)

Values in this register correspond to 4 pins on the EEPROM:
- Bit 0: Data Out (DO)
- Bit 1: Data In (DI)
- Bit 6: Clock (CLK or SK in existing code)
- Bit 7: Chip Select (CS)

The other pins (notably ORG, which controls 8-bit vs 16-bit addressing) do not appear to be connected to this register.

Commands are sent to the EEPROM by shifting in a bitstream to DI while manually clocking CLK. All commands must be preceded by a 1 bit, and existing games precede the 1 bit with a 0 bit (though this is not necessary):
- Write $00 (lower CS)
- Write $80 (raise CS)
- Write $C0 (shift in 0 bit)
- Write $82 (lower CS, raise DI)
- Write $C2 (shift in 1 bit)
- Write command

text:
## EEPROM Commands

The following commands exist, each 10 bits (excluding data shifted in or out). "x" means the value of this bit is ignored. "A" means the relevant bit of the address. All data is shifted in or out MSB first. Note that data is addressed 16 bits at a time, so address 1 corresponds to bits 16-31, thus bytes 2-3.

| Command | Bit Pattern |
|---|---|
| READ | 10xAAAAAAAb (then shift out 16 bits) |
| EWEN (Erase/Write enable) | 0011xxxxxxb |
| EWDS (Erase/Write disable) | 0000xxxxxxb |
| WRITE | 01xAAAAAAAb (then shift in 16 bits) |
| ERASE (fill address with $FFFF) | 11xAAAAAAAb |
| ERAL (fill EEPROM with $FFFF) | 0010xxxxxxb |
| WRAL (fill EEPROM with value) | 0001xxxxxxb (then shift in 16 bits) |

All programming operations (WRITE/ERASE/WRAL/ERAL) must be preceded with EWEN.

According to the datasheet, programming operations take time to settle. Continue clocking and check the value of DO to verify if command is still running. Data sheet says that the signal to DO is RDY, thus it reads a 1 when the command finishes.

## MBC7 Control Registers

### $0000-1FFF — RAM Enable 1 (Write Only)
Mostly the same as for MBC1, a value of $0A will enable reading and writing to RAM registers. A value of $00 will disable it. The RAM must second be enabled in the second RAM enable section as well ($4000-5FFF).

### $2000-3FFF — ROM Bank Number (Write Only)
The ROM bank number goes here.

### $4000-5FFF — RAM Enable 2 (Write Only)
Writing $40 to this region enables access to the RAM registers. Writing any other value appears to disable access to RAM, but this is not fully tested. The RAM must first be enabled in the first RAM enable section as well ($0000-1FFF).

text:
# HuC-3

HuC-3 is an MBC developed by Hudson Soft. Besides ROM and RAM banking, it also provides a real-time clock, speaker, and infrared communication. The CR2025 coin cell is user-replaceable. It is a successor to the HuC1.

The HuC-3 is poorly understood. Observed behavior suggests the real-time clock and tone generator are implemented using a 4-bit microcontroller core with internal program ROM.

## Memory

### $0000–3FFF — ROM Bank 00 [read-only]
Contains the first 16 KiB of the ROM.

### $4000–7FFF — ROM Bank 00–7F [read-only]
This area may contain any of the further 16 KiB banks of the ROM. Like the MBC5, bank $00 can also be mapped to this region.

### $A000–BFFF — RAM Bank 00–03, or RTC/IR register [read/write]
Depending on the current register selection and RAM Bank Number (see below), this memory space is used to access an 8 KiB external RAM Bank, or a single I/O Register.

text:
## Memory Control Registers

### $0000–1FFF — RAM/RTC/IR Select [read/write]
Writing to this register maps cart RAM, RTC registers or IR registers into memory at $A000–BFFF. Only the lower 4 bits are significant.

| Value | Feature |
|---|---|
| $0 | Cart RAM (read-only) |
| $A | Cart RAM (read/write) |
| $B | RTC command/argument (write) |
| $C | RTC command/response (read) |
| $D | RTC semaphore (read/write) |
| $E | IR (read/write) |

If any other value is written, reads from $A000–BFFF return open bus values (often $FF, but not guaranteed), and writes are ignored.

### $2000–3FFF — ROM Bank Number [write-only]
HuC-3 can accept a 7-bit bank number here.

### $4000–5FFF — RAM Bank Select [write-only]
HuC-3 can accept a bank number of at least 2 bits here.

### $6000–7FFF — Nothing? [write-only]
Games write $01 here on startup, but it doesn't seem to have any observable effect.

text:
## I/O Registers

These registers are accessed by reading or writing in the $A000–BFFF range after setting the RAM/RTC/IR Select register. Address lines A12–A0 are not connected to the HuC-3 chip, so the offset within the range is ignored. Data line D7 is not connected to the HuC-3 chip, so the most significant bit of reads always returns an open bus value (usually high), and the most significant bit of writes is always ignored.

The RTC MCU communication protocol is described below.

### $B — RTC Command/Argument [write]
The value written consists of a command in bits 6-4, and an argument value in bits 3–0. For example the value $62 is command $6 with argument value $2. Writing to this register just sets the values in the mailbox registers – it does not cause the command to be executed.

### $C — RTC Command/Response [read]
When read, bits 6–4 return the last command written to register $B, and bits 3–0 contain the result from the last command executed.

### $D — RTC Semaphore [read/write]
When reading, the least significant bit is high when the RTC MCU is ready to receive a command, or low when the RTC MCU is busy. Writing with the least significant bit clear requests that the RTC MCU execute the last command written to register $B.

### $E — IR [read/write]
Similar to the equivalent register of the HuC1. The least significant bit is used for infrared communication.

text:
## RTC Communication Protocol

The HuC-3 chip provides read and write access to a 256-nybble window into the RTC MCU's internal memory. Multi-nybble values are stored with the least significant nybble at the lowest address (little Endian). There are commands for setting the access address, reading and writing values, and a few higher-level operations.

Games use the following sequence to execute a command:
* Write $0D to $0000 (select RTC Semaphore register)
* Poll $A000 until least significant bit is set (wait until ready)
* Write $0B to $0000 (select RTC Command/Argument register)
* Write command and argument to $A000
* Write $0D to $0000 (select RTC semaphore register)
* Write $FE to $A000 (clear semaphore, requesting MCU execute command)
* Poll $A000 until least significant bit is set (wait for completion)
* Write $0C to $0000 (select RTC Command/Response register)
* Read $A000 and use value from 4 least significant bits

(The last two steps are not applicable for commands that don't produce a response.)

Known commands:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| $1 | Read value and increment access address |
| $3 | Write value and increment access address |
| $4 | Set access address least significant nybble |
| $5 | Set access address most significant nybble |
| $6 | Execute extended command specified by argument |

Pocket Family GB2 uses command $2 with argument $0, its purpose is unknown. Commands $0 and $7 have not been observed.

Extended commands:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| $0 | Copy current time to locations $00–06 |
| $1 | Copy locations $00–06 to current time, and update event time |
| $2 | Seems to be some kind of status request |
| $E | Executing twice triggers tone generator |

Commands $0 and $1 are used to read and write the current time atomically. Writing the current time in this way also updates the event time to maintain the remaining duration until the event occurs.
Command $2 is used on start and seems to be some kind of status request. The games will not start if the result is not $1.

### RTC Location Map
| Location | Purpose |
|---|---|
| $00–06 | Output or input space for reading or writing current time |
| $10–12 | Minute of day counter (rolls over at 1440) |
| $13–15 | Day counter |
| $26 | Tone selection in two least significant bits |
| $27 | Tone generator enabled if value is 1 (all bits checked) |
| $58–5A | Event time minutes |
| $5B–5D | Event time days |

text:
# M161

The M161 is a simple multi-cart mapper by Mani (Nintendo's official Chinese distributor). This mapper only allows for 8 separate 32 KiB banks, with a limit of 1 bankswitch, preventing any bankswitches afterward. This gives 1 bank for the main menu, and a maximum of 7 different 32 KiB games.
This mapper does not support SRAM, so only purely MBC-less titles can be used.

This mapper is only known to be used in a single cartridge: Mani's 4 in 1 cartridge, which contains Tetris, Tennis, Alleyway, and Yakuman. All later Mani 4 in 1 cartridges use the MMM01 mapper, so the M161 mapper could be considered the predecessor to the MMM01.

The mapper is really just a simple off-the-shelf 74HC161A used as a flip-flop. The 74HC161A contains 4 input pins and 4 output pins, and another pin for latching the input pins to the output pins. The first 3 of the input pins are connected to the lower 3 bits of incoming write data, with the 4th pin kept high. The first 3 of the output pins are connected to the ROM addressing pins (RA15-RA17). The 4th output pin (which corresponds to the always high input pin) is connected to the latching pin. Bit 15 of the write address is also connected to (OR'd with) the latching pin. The latching pin latches the input pins to the output pins on a transition from high to low, so when a ROM write comes through (bit 15 = 0), the input pins will be latched to the output pins. Since 4th output pin will be high, the signal cannot become low anymore, thus preventing further bankswitches.

## Memory
### $0000-7FFF — ROM Bank $00-$07 [read only]
This area contains a 32 KiB bank of ROM. On startup, this will contain the first 32 KiB of ROM (bank 0).

## Registers
### $0000-7FFF — ROM Bank Number [write only]
This 3-bit register (range $00-$07) selects the ROM bank number for the $0000-$7FFF region. Like other mappers, the high bits are discarded.
Only 1 bankswitch is allowed per session; once any write to this register occurs (even if set to 0), all future writes will be ignored until the mapper is powered down.

## Operation Notes
Because the full 32 KiB range is switched over, caution should be taken, as the entire ROM range will change. It may be best to have the bankswitch code placed right before $0100, so as to fall through to the game's init sequence.

text:
# MMM01

The MMM01 is a mapper specific to multi-game compilation cartridges. It emulates an MBC1 for the contained games, and supports containing a mix of games from 32 KiB ROMs with no RAM, up to the same maximum memory per game as the MBC1:
- max 512 KiB ROM with banked 8, 16, or 32 KiB RAM (default configuration)
- max 2 MiB ROM with unbanked 8 KiB RAM ("multiplex" mode, never used commercially)

Regardless of the size or number of the games in the compilation, the maximum total cartridge size supported by the MMM01 is the same: up to 8 MiB ROM and 128 KiB RAM.

The ROM and RAM banking numbers are extended compared to the MBC1 to allow for game selection, and the lower bits (equivalent to the MBC1 bank registers) can be masked so some of those bits can also be used for game selection (for smaller games). This allows up to 255x 32 KiB games, plus a 32 KiB menu, in an 8 MiB ROM. RAM is more limited at only up to 16x 8 KiB RAM banks.
However, despite these generous capabilities, no MMM01 cartridge was released with more than 4 games, and only one contains any RAM at all.

The ROM and RAM "game select" banking bits do not have to be set to the same value; this allows an MMM01 cartridge to not waste RAM space on games that do not have RAM, or mix and match games that have differently-sized ROM or RAM by packing them in tightly in the overall ROM/RAM of the cartridge.

:::warning
The MMM01 can't completely block access to RAM for a game, so if the cartridge contains RAM it's recommended to assign any no-RAM games to the same single RAM bank to prevent no-RAM games from accessing or corrupting other games' save RAM.
:::

MMM01 starts up in a unique mode ("unmapped") which always maps the last 32 KiB of the ROM to the $0000-7FFF address region regardless of the values in the bank/mode registers. The correct ROM header (with Nintendo logo) therefore needs to be located at offset `(size - 32 KiB) + $100` in the ROM rather than the usual `$0000 + $100` (which contains the header of the first game in the collection instead). MMM01 cartridges have a simple menu program in this last 32 KiB, which manipulates the additional MMM01 control bits, allowing game selection and setting the game size, before entering "mapped" mode and booting the selected game.

As the last 32 KiB of the ROM are reserved for the cartridge menu, it's best to pack games into the ROM from largest to smallest to avoid having a game overlap the menu. For example, the Taito Variety Pack contains three 128 KiB games and one 64 KiB game in a 512 KiB ROM chip, leaving 32 KiB unused and 32 KiB for the menu.

text:
## Memory

### $0000-3FFF — ROM Bank $X0 (Read Only)
On startup (in "unmapped" mode), this is mapped to the first half of the menu program in the last 32 KiB of the ROM.
When a game is mapped, this area normally contains the first 16 KiB (bank 00) of the game ROM.
If multiplex is enabled, entering mode 1 allows mapping game ROM banks $20, $40, and $60 to this region.

### $4000-7FFF — ROM Bank $01-7F (Read Only)
On startup (in "unmapped" mode), this is mapped to the second half of the menu program in the last 32 KiB of the ROM.
When a game is mapped, this area may contain any of the further 16 KiB banks of the game ROM, except for game banks $00, $20, $40, or $60. If one of those banks is selected, the low bit is forced to 1 and that bank is mapped instead ($01, $21, $41, or $61).

i.e. in mapped mode, if `(ROM Bank Low) & ~(ROM Bank Mask)` is equal to $00 (indicating bank $00 within the game ROM), `(ROM Bank Low) | 1` is used instead.
As an example, if ROM Bank Low is set to $10, and the ROM Bank Mask is set to $30, then the bank within the game ROM would be `($10) & ~($30) = $00`. As game bank $00 is disallowed, the low bit is forced on, mapping bank $11 instead of $10.

If multiplex is enabled, the MMM01 has the same limitation as MBC1 regarding accessing game ROM banks $20, $40, and $60 — they can only be mapped to $0000-3FFF (in mode 1), and not to $4000-7FFF.

### $A000-BFFF — RAM Bank $00-03, if any (Read/Write)
This area is used to address external SRAM in the cartridge (if any). The RAM is only accessible if RAM is enabled, otherwise reads return open bus values (often $FF, but not guaranteed) and writes are ignored.
External RAM is often battery-backed, allowing for the storage of game data while the Game Boy is turned off, or if the cartridge is removed from the Game Boy.
It is currently unknown whether RAM access is possible while in unmapped mode.

text:
## Registers

The MMM01 registers are extensions of the MBC1 registers. In "mapped" mode, the extra bits cannot be written to, and the MMM01 emulates an MBC1. In "unmapped" mode, the extra bits are writeable, allowing the menu program to select which game to play and configure its size and RAM access.
All the MMM01 registers are 7-bits in size and set to $00 on power-up. For the ROM Bank Number register, this behaves as if it was set to $01.

### $0000-1FFF — RAM Enable (Write Only)
Bits 0-3: RAM Enable — As per MBC1, writing $A to the lower 4 bits enables the external RAM, and any other value disables it. The external RAM is automatically disabled when the Game Boy is powered off or the cartridge is removed.

Bits 4-5: RAM Bank Mask (unmapped mode only) — These two bits act as "write locks" to the matching bits in the RAM Bank Low register. Any attempt to write to those bits while its matching bit in this register is 1 is prevented. Writes to masked bits are prevented even in "unmapped" mode.
The purpose of the mask is to lock some RAM banking bits as "game selection", instead of letting games freely toggle them.

| Mask | Game RAM (default) | Game ROM (multiplex) |
|---|---|---|
| 00 | 32 KiB | 2 MiB |
| 10 | 16 KiB | 1 MiB |
| 11 | 8 KiB | 512 KiB* |

Setting the RAM Bank Mask to 11 when multiplex is enabled is pointless — the whole point of multiplex mode is to support 1 MiB or larger MBC1 games.

Bit 6: Mapping Enable (unmapped mode only) — When writing 1 to this bit, the MMM01 enters "mapped" mode. This immediately begins MBC1 emulation, mapping in the selected banks of the game ROM and prevents write access to any of the MMM01 extended control bits.
It is unknown if setting bit 6 to enter "mapped" mode will honor or ignore the value simultaneously being written to bits 4-5 (RAM Bank Mask). The only released MMM01 cartridge containing RAM performs two separate writes to set the bank mask before enabling mapping, with the same mask set in both writes.

text:
### $2000-3FFF — ROM Bank Number (Write Only)
Bits 0-4: ROM Bank Low — This is equivalent to the MBC1 ROM Bank register, and primarily selects which bank of the game ROM is visible in the $4000-7FFF region. It can be masked to reduce its size, reserving some bits for game select (see ROM Bank Mask).
If the unmasked bits are all 0, it behaves as if the lowest bit is set to 1 (as per MBC1 behaviour, attempting to map bank $00 of the game ROM maps bank $01 instead). However, the actual value of the register doesn't change; changes to ROM Bank Mask can undo this remapping.

Bits 5-6: ROM Bank Mid (unmapped mode only) — This register represents an additional two bits of ROM bank number, for game selection. Affects both the $0000-3FFF and $4000-7FFF region. Can only be used for game selection, as it's not writeable once entering a game (mapped mode).
If multiplex is enabled, functionality is swapped with RAM Bank Low allowing for larger game ROM.

### $4000-5FFF — RAM Bank Number (Write Only)
Bits 0-1: RAM Bank Low — This is equivalent to the MBC1 RAM Bank register. It can be masked to reduce its size, reserving some bits for game select (see RAM Bank Mask).
If multiplex is enabled, functionality is swapped with ROM Bank Mid allowing for larger game ROM.

Bits 2-3: RAM Bank High (unmapped mode only) — This register represents an additional two bits of RAM bank number, for game selection. Can only be used for game selection, as it's not writeable once entering a game (mapped mode).

Bits 4-5: ROM Bank High (unmapped mode only) — This register represents another two bits of ROM bank number, for game selection. Affects both the $0000-3FFF and $4000-7FFF region. Can only be used for game selection, as it's not writeable once entering a game (mapped mode).

Bit 6: MBC1 Mode Write Lock (unmapped mode only) — When set to 1, prevents changes to the MBC1 Mode Select. This might be for compatibility with games designed for non-MBC1 mappers that don't expect the MBC1 mode select register to exist.

text:
### $6000-7FFF — Banking Mode Select (Write Only)

Bit 0: MBC1 Mode Select — This is equivalent to the MBC1 Mode Select register. This 1-bit register selects between the two MBC1 banking modes.

**Multiplex disabled:**
- 0 = RAM Banking Disabled (default). In mode 0, the $A000-BFFF region is locked to bank 0 of the game RAM. The unmasked bits of RAM Bank Low are treated as 0.
- 1 = RAM Banking Enabled. In mode 1, the $A000-BFFF region can be bank-switched by the game as the full RAM Bank Low register is used.

**Multiplex enabled:**
- 0 = Simple ROM Banking Mode (default). In mode 0, the $0000-3FFF region is locked to bank 0 of the game ROM. The unmasked bits of RAM Bank Low are treated as 0 for accesses to the $0000-3FFF region.
- 1 = Advanced ROM Banking Mode. In mode 1, the $0000-3FFF region can be bank-switched using the RAM Bank Low register — allowing access to game ROM banks $20, $40, and $60.

Bits 1-5: ROM Bank Mask (unmapped mode only) — These five bits act as "write locks" to the matching bits in the ROM Bank Low register. Any attempt to write to the bits in ROM Bank Low while its matching bit in this register is 1 is prevented. Writes to masked bits are prevented even in "unmapped" mode.
The value written to the lowest bit of the mask is ignored, and treated as always zero. As a result, the lowest bit of ROM Bank Low is always writeable.

| Mask | Game ROM |
|---|---|
| 00000 | 512 KiB |
| 10000 | 256 KiB |
| 11000 | 128 KiB |
| 11100 | 64 KiB |
| 11110 | 32 KiB |

Note: changing the mask can alter which bank would be mapped. Only the unmasked bits of ROM Bank Low are used for the "attempting to map bank 0 maps bank 1" logic, and it updates live if the ROM Bank Mask changes. ROM Bank Low itself doesn't change when this happens — only the value used for calculating the bank number.
If multiplex is enabled, the RAM Bank Mask affects ROM banking as well. In this case the ROM Bank Mask should be set to 00000 to avoid masking bits in the middle of the full game ROM bank number.

Multiplex Enable (unmapped mode only) — When set to 1, swaps the functionality of RAM Bank Low and ROM Bank Mid. As RAM Bank Low is writeable in mapped mode, this allows for the contained game to control (up to, if unmasked in "RAM Bank Mask") two extra bits of the full ROM bank number, allowing for larger game ROMs at the cost of only 8 KiB of external RAM. This is equivalent to the "large ROM" wiring of an MBC1 cartridge.

## Operation Notes
Because the MMM01 has registers which disable write access to other registers, it is optimal to set the value of certain registers before others. The optimal order is:
1. ROM Bank Register ($2000) — contains ROM Bank Low and Mid
2. Mode Register ($6000) — contains ROM Bank Mask, and Mode
3. RAM Register ($4000) — contains Mode Write Disable, and RAM Bank
4. RAM Enable ($0000) — contains RAM Bank Mask, and Mapping Enable
The majority of released MMM01 cartridges stick to this order.

text:
# Other MBCs

## Multicart MBCs
**MBC1M** uses the MBC1 IC, but the board does not connect the MBC1's A18 address output to the ROM. This allows including multiple 2 Mbit (16 bank) games, with SRAM bank select ($4000) to select which of up to four games is switched in. In theory, a MBC1M board could be made for 1 Mbit or 512 kbit games by additionally not connecting A17 and A16 outputs, but this appears not to have been done in licensed games.
**Bung** and **EMS** MBCs are reported to exist.

## EMS
:::warning To be verified
Take the following with a grain of salt, as it hasn't been verified on authentic EMS hardware. See related github issue to contribute: #423.
:::

A header matching any of the following is detected as EMS mapper:
- Header name is "EMSMENU", NUL-padded
- Header name is "GB16M", NUL-padded
- Cartridge type ($0147) = $1B and region ($014A) = $E1

Registers:
- $2000 write: Normal behavior, plus save written value in $2000 latch
- $1000 write: $A5 enables configure mode, $98 disables it, and other values have no known effect
- $7000 write while configure mode is on: Copy $2000 latch to OR mask

After the OR mask has been set, all reads from ROM will OR A21-A14 (the bank number) with the OR mask. This chooses which game is visible to the CPU. If the OR mask is not aligned to the game size, the results may be nonsensical.
The mapper does not support an outer bank for battery SRAM.

To start a game, perform the following steps with code running from RAM:
1. Write $A5 to $1000
2. Write game's first bank number to $2000
3. Write any value to $7000
4. Write $98 to $1000
5. Write $01 to $2000 (so that 32K games work)
6. Jump to $0100

## Wisdom Tree
The Wisdom Tree mapper is a simple, cost-optimized one-chip design consisting of a 74LS377 octal latch in addition to the ROM chip. Because the mapper consists of a single standard 74 series logic chip, it has two unusual properties:

First, unlike a usual MBC, it switches the whole 32 KiB ROM area instead of just the $4000-$7FFF area. Therefore, if you want to use the interrupt vectors with this cart, you should duplicate them across all banks. Additionally, since the 74LS377's contents can't be guaranteed when powering on, the ROM header and some code for switching to a known bank should also be included in every bank. This also means that the Wisdom Tree mapper could be used as a multicart mapper for 32 KiB ROMs, assuming there is enough ROM space in each bank for some small initialization code, and none of the ROMs wrote to the $0000-$7FFF area.

Second, because the 74LS377 latches data on the positive write pulse edge, and the value on the Game Boy data bus is no longer valid when the positive edge arrives, the designer of this mapper chose to use the A7-A0 address lines for selecting a bank instead of the data lines. Thus, the value you write is ignored, and the lower 8 bits of the address is used. For example, to select bank $XX, you would write any value to address $YYXX, where $YY is in the range $00-$7F.

text:
## Magic values for detection of multicarts in emulators

:::tip proposal
The following information should not be considered a universally adopted standard, but it's instead just a proposed solution. Actual adoption may vary.
:::

Sometimes it may be useful to allow a ROM to be detected as a multicart in emulator, for example for development of a menu for physical multicart hardware.
Emulator authors who are interested in supporting the other multicart mappers are also encouraged to support detection of the following values.

- Detect as Wisdom Tree mapper:
  - ROM title is "WISDOM TREE" (the space may be a $00 NUL character instead), $0147 = $00, $0148 = $00, size > 32k. This method works for the games released by Wisdom Tree, Inc.
  - $0147 = $C0, $014A = $D1.
- Detect as EMS multicart: $0147 = $1B, $014A = $E1
- Detect as Bung multicart: $0147 = $BE

text:
# GBC Infrared Communication

The Game Boy Color came with an infrared port on the very top of the handheld. Previously, where IR communications had to be done with special cartridges (like the HuC-1 variants), the Game Boy itself now had the hardware built-in. Unfortunately, the feature was never popular outside of a few games and accessories. The IR port essentially sends out signals and is also capable of receiving them, allowing for fast, wireless, line-of-sight transmission.

- GBC comes with one IR port. Capable of sending and receiving an IR signal (two separate diodes).
- Turning on the IR light does drain battery; hence not recommended leaving it on when not in use.
- IR port can communicate with non-GBC devices, e.g. anything that sends an IR signal (TV remotes, Wiimotes, household lamps, etc).

## Communication Types
While a number of games may use similar formats for their IR communications, there is no "standard" protocol that all games use. IR communication is entirely determined by the game's code.

- **1-Player Init**: Only require one GBC to initiate IR transfers. Both GBCs typically wait for an infrared signal. When one player presses a button, the GBC starts sending pulses. This setup is not unlike how 2-Player Serial I/O is handled (with master and slave Game Boys). Examples include Super Mario Bros. DX score exchange and the GBC-to-GBC Mystery Gifts in Pokemon Gold/Silver/Crystal. Most IR compatible games fall into this group.
- **2-Player Init**: Transfers require both GBCs to initiate at roughly the same time. Examples include Pokemon Pinball score exchange, Pokemon TCG's "Card Pop", and trading/fighting Charaboms in the Bomberman games.
- **Active Object Init**: Transfers require the GBC to interact with another non-GBC device capable of both sending and receiving infrared signals. These objects are designed to work specifically with GBCs and send pulses in much the same manner as a GBC would. Examples include Mystery Gifts via the Pokemon Pikachu 2 and trading Points via the Pocket Sakura.
- **Inactive Object Init**: Transfers require the GBC to interact with another non-GBC device capable of sending infrared signals but not necessarily receiving them. These objects may not be designed to work specifically with GBCs (notable exception is the Full Changer). Communication is input-only for these cases. Examples include Zok Zok Heroes, Chee Chai Alien, the Bomberman Max games' special stages, and Mission Impossible's TV remote feature.

text:
## Communication Protocol
Again, there is no set or established infrared protocol that games must follow. Many games vary in their approach. For example, the 2nd Generation Pokemon games use the GBC's hardware timers, while others have hardcoded values that count cycles to check timing. The simplest form is a bare-bones communication protocol, i.e. something like a binary Morse code where a "0" is a long ON-OFF pulse and "1" is a short ON-OFF pulse or vice versa.

One thing to note is that 4 games in particular do share somewhat similar IR protocols, at least regarding the initial handshake between 2 GBCs. They are Pokemon TCG 1 & 2 and Bomberman Max Red & Blue, all from the "2-Player Init" category above. Typically, IR capable GBC games will continually wait for an IR signal on both sides (the "1-Player Init" category). The handshake for these 4 games requires both players to input at almost the same time. One has to be slightly faster or slower than the other. Each side continually sends a few IR pulses, then reads the sensor to see if anything was received. If so, the GBCs begin to sync.

## RP Register ($FF56)
The MMIO register located at $FF56 controls infrared communication. Simply known as "RP" (Radiation Port? Reception Port? Red Port???), it is responsible for sending and receiving IR signals.

| Bit(s) | Effect | Access |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Turn IR light ON (1) or OFF (0) | R/W |
| 1 | Bit 1 = 1 | R |
| 2-5 | Unused | |
| 6-7 | Signal Read Enable (0 = Disable) (3 = Enable) | R/W |

Turning on the IR light is as simple as writing to Bit 0 of RP. Reading is a bit more complicated. Bits 6 and 7 must both be set ($C0), to read Bit 1; otherwise Bit 1 returns 1, acting as if no signal is detected.

## Signal Fade
The IR sensor in the GBC adapts to the current level of IR light. If the GBC receives a sustained IR signal beyond a certain amount of time, eventually the sensor treats this as a new "normal" level of IR light, and Bit 1 of RP goes back to 1. This is called the signal "fade" because it may appear as if the signal disappears.

Signal fade time is dependent on length and has an inverse relationship with the distance between a GBC and the IR light. The closer a GBC is to the IR source, the longer the fade time. At about 3.0 to 3.5 inches (7.62 to 8.89 cm) signal fade time appears to be around 3ms. Optimal distance seems to be 2.5 to 4.0 inches (6.35 to 10.16 cm). Putting two GBCs very close together (physically touching) produced unusually short fade times, far shorter than 3ms.

## Obscure Behavior
The RP register has one very strange quirk. Disabling Bits 6 and 7 and then subsequently re-enabling them causes Bit 1 to go to zero under certain conditions. In other words, the IR sensor will act as if it is detecting a signal if reading the signal is disabled then enabled.

| Result of 1st RP Write ($00) | Result of 2nd RP Write ($C0) | Type of Lighting |
|---|---|---|
| Bit 1 = 1 | Bit 1 = 1 | Dark |
| Bit 1 = 0 | Bit 1 = 1 | Ambient |
| Bit 1 = 0 (sometimes 1) | Bit 1 = 0 | Bright |

Writing $00 to RP, followed by $C0 will trigger these results. When enabling Bits 6 and 7 (writing $C0), it takes some time for the sensor to register legitimate IR light coming into the sensor. Generally a few hundred cycles in double-speed mode will suffice. If Bit 1 of RP remains 1 after the loop, it's safe to assume the lighting is either ambient or dark.

text:
# SGB Description

## General Description
The Super Game Boy (SGB) is an adapter cartridge that allows to play Game Boy games on a SNES (Super Nintendo Entertainment System) gaming console. In detail, you plug the Game Boy cartridge into the SGB cartridge, then plug the SGB cartridge into the SNES, and then connect the SNES to your TV Set. In result, games can be played and viewed on the TV Set, and are controlled by using the SNES joypad(s).

## More Technical Description
The SGB cartridge contains a Game Boy system on chip, with its normal CPU and video and sound controller. It also has a bridge circuit, the ICD2, to translate joypad input, video signal, and control packets between the SNES and GB as well as a system software ROM that runs on the SNES. The system software forwards button presses from the controllers and sends the video signal to the TV set, giving both the player and the game a small amount of control over the appearance.

## Normal Monochrome Games
Any Game Boy games which have been designed for monochrome handheld Game Boy systems will work with the SGB hardware as well. The SGB will apply a four color palette to these games by replacing the normal four shades of gray. The 160×144 pixel game screen is displayed in the middle of the 256×224 pixel SNES screen (the unused area is filled by a screen border bitmap). The user may access built-in menus, allowing to change color palette data, to select between several pre-defined borders, etc.

text:
## SGB Features

**Colorized Game Screen**: There's limited ability to colorize the game screen by assigning custom color palettes to each 20×18 display characters. However, this works mainly for static display data such as title screens or status bars; the 20×18 color attribute map cannot be scrolled, and it is not possible to assign separate colors to moveable foreground objects (sprites), so animated screen regions will typically be restricted to using a single palette of four colors only.

**SNES Foreground Sprites**: Up to 24 additional 16-color objects of 8×8 or 16×16 pixels can be displayed. When replacing (or just overlaying) the normal Game Boy objects by SNES objects, it'd be possible to display objects with other colors than normal background area. This method doesn't appear to be very popular. The bottommost character line of the game screen will be masked out because this area is used to transfer object positions to the SNES. (Later versions of the SGB system software remove much of this object enhancement.)

**The SGB Border**: The possibly most popular and most impressive feature is to replace the default SGB screen border by a custom bitmap which is stored in the game cartridge. A border can be much more colorful than the game screen, as it uses 4 bits per pixel (16 color per tile) instead of 2 bpp (4 colors/tile).

**Multiple Joypads**: Up to four joypads can be connected to the SNES, and SGB software may read-out each of these joypads separately, allowing up to four players to play the same game simultaneously. Unlike for multiplayer handheld games, this requires only one game cartridge and only one SGB/SNES, and no link cables are required; the downside is that all players must share the same display screen.

**Sound Functions**: Alongside normal Game Boy sound, a number of digital sound effects is pre-defined in the SNES BIOS. Programmers familiar with SNES sounds may also access the SNES sound chip, or use the SNES "Kankichi" sequencer engine directly to produce other sound effects or music.

**Taking Control of the SNES CPU**: It is possible to write program code or data into SNES memory, and to execute such program code by using the SNES CPU.

## SGB Clock Speed
Because the SGB is synchronized to the SNES CPU, the Game Boy Master Clock is directly chained to the SNES Master Clock. In result, the Game Boy CPU, video controller, timers, and sound frequencies will all operate approx 2.4% faster than handheld systems.

- NTSC SGB: 21.477 MHz Master Clock, 4.2955 MHz GB Master Clock, 2.41% fast
- PAL SGB: 21.281 MHz Master Clock, 4.2563 MHz GB Master Clock, 1.48% fast
- NTSC SGB2: Separate 20.972 MHz crystal, correct speed

Sensitive musicians may notice that sound frequencies are a bit too high, particularly in programs that use GB sound alongside Kankichi. Programs that support SGB functions may avoid this effect by reducing frequencies of Game Boy sounds when having detected SGB hardware. Also, "PAL version" SNES models which use a 50Hz display refresh rate (rather than 60Hz) result in respectively slower Game Boy timings.

text:
# Unlocking and Detecting SGB Functions

## Cartridge Header
SGB games are required to have a cartridge header with Nintendo logo and proper checksum just as normal Game Boy games. Also, two special entries must be set in order to unlock SGB functions:
- **SGB flag**: Must be set to $03 for SGB games
- **Old licensee code**: Must be set to $33 for SGB games

When these entries aren't set, the game will still work just like all "monochrome" Game Boy games, but it cannot access any of the special SGB functions.

## Detecting SGB hardware
SGB hardware can be detected by examining the initial value of the C register directly after startup: a value of $14 indicates SGB or SGB2 hardware. It is also possible to separate between SGB and SGB2 by examining the initial value of the A register directly after startup.

| Console | A Register | C Register |
|---|---|---|
| DMG | $01 | $13 |
| SGB | $01 | $14 |
| MGB | $FF | $13 |
| SGB2 | $FF | $14 |
| CGB | $11 | $00 |
| AGB | $11 | $00 |

The DMG and MGB share initial A register values with the SGB and SGB2 respectively.
The SGB2 doesn't have any extra features which'd require separate SGB2 detection except for curiosity purposes; for example, "Tetris DX" chooses to display an alternate SGB border on SGB2s. Only the SGB2 contains a link port.

SGB hardware has traditionally been detected by sending MLT_REQ commands, but this method is more complicated and slower than checking the value of the A and C registers after startup. The MLT_REQ command enables two (or four) joypads; a normal handheld Game Boy will ignore this command, but an SGB will return incrementing joypad IDs each time when deselecting keypad lines. The joypad state/IDs can then be read out several times, and if the IDs are changing, then it is an SGB (a normal Game Boy would typically always return $0F as the ID). Finally, when not intending to use more than one joypad, send another MLT_REQ command in order to disable the multi-controller mode. Detection works regardless of how many joypads are physically connected to the SNES. However, unlike the C register method, this detection works only when SGB functions are unlocked from the cartridge header.

text:
# Command Packet Transfers

Command packets are transferred from the Game Boy to the SNES by using bits 4 and 5 of the JOYP register ($FF00). These lines are normally used to select one of the two button groups (which still works as usual).

## Transferring Bits
A command packet transfer must be initiated by setting JOYP bits 4 and 5 both to 0; this will reset and start the ICD2 packet receiving circuit. Data is then transferred (LSB first); setting bit 4 to 0 will indicate a `0` bit, and setting bit 5 to 0 will indicate a `1` bit.

The boot ROM and licensed software keep data and reset pulses LOW for at least 5 M-cycles and leave bit 4 and 5 both to 1 for at least 15 M-cycles after each pulse. Though the hardware is capable of receiving pulses and spaces as short as 2 M-cycles, following the common practice of 5 M-cycle pulses and 15 M-cycle spaces may improve reliability.

:::tip Obviously, it'd be no good idea to modify the joypad register in the middle of a transfer. For example, if your VBlank interrupt procedure normally reads out button states each frame, you should disable that behavior using a variable (or disable the interrupt handler entirely).
:::

:::warning The GB program should wait 60 ms (4 frames) between each packet transfer and the next, as the "bomb" tool to erase a user-drawn border can cause the SGB system software not to check for packets for 4 frames.
:::

## Packet format
Each packet transfer is started by a "Start" pulse, then 16 bytes (128 bits) of data are transferred, the LSB of each byte first; and finally, a `0` bit must be transferred as a stop bit. These 130 bit periods correspond to at least 2600 M-cycles at the recommended rate.

The structure of the first packet in a transmission is:
1. 1 pulse: Start signal
2. 1 byte: Header byte (Command Code × 8 + Length)
3. 15 bytes: Data
4. 1 bit: Stop Bit (`0`)

The above Length indicates the total number of packets (1-7, including the first packet) which will be sent. If more than 15 data bytes are used, then further packet(s) will follow:
1. 1 pulse: Start signal
2. 16 bytes: Data
3. 1 bit: Stop Bit (`0`)

By using all 7 packets, up to 111 data bytes (15 + 16 × 6) may be sent.
:::tip Bytes with no indicated purpose are simply ignored by the SGB BIOS. They can be set to any value (but they must still be transferred).
:::

text:
# System Control Commands

## SGB Command $17 — MASK_EN
Used to mask the Game Boy window; among others this can be used to freeze the Game Boy screen before transferring data through VRAM (the SNES then keeps displaying the Game Boy screen, even though VRAM doesn't contain meaningful display information during the transfer).

| Byte | Content |
|---|---|
| 0 | Command*8+Length (fixed length=1) |
| 1 | Game Boy Screen Mask (0-3): 0=Cancel Mask (Display activated), 1=Freeze Screen, 2=Blank Screen (Black), 3=Blank Screen (Color 0) |
| 2-F | Not used (zero) |

Freezing works only if the SNES has stored a picture; wait one or two frames before freezing. The Cancel Mask function may also be invoked by completion of PAL_SET and ATTR_SET commands.
:::tip SGB freezes/unfreezes the Game Boy screen automatically whenever the LCD is turned off/on.
:::

## SGB Command $0C — ATRC_EN
Used to enable/disable Attraction mode, which is enabled by default. Built-in borders other than the Game Boy frame and the plain black border have a "screen saver" activated by pressing R, L, L, L, L, R or by leaving the controller alone for roughly 7 minutes (tested with 144p Test Suite). It is speculated that the animation may have interfered with rarely-used SGB features such as OBJ_TRN or JUMP.

| Byte | Content |
|---|---|
| 0 | Command*8+Length (fixed length=1) |
| 1 | Attraction Disable: 0=Enable, 1=Disable |
| 2-F | Not used (zero) |

## SGB Command $0E — ICON_EN
Used to enable/disable ICON function. Possibly meant to enable/disable SGB/SNES popup menus which might otherwise be activated during Game Boy game play. By default all functions are enabled (0).

| Byte | Content |
|---|---|
| 0 | Command*8+Length (fixed length=1) |
| 1 | Disable Bits: Bit 0=SGB-Built-in Color Palettes (1=Disable), Bit 1=Controller Set-up Screen (1=Disable), Bit 2=SGB Register File Transfer (1=Disable), Bit 3-6=Not used (zero) |
| 2-F | Not used (zero) |

Bit 2 will suppress all further packets/commands when set; this might be useful when starting a monochrome game from inside the SGB-menu of a multi-gamepak.

text:
## SGB Command $0F — DATA_SND
Used to write one or more bytes directly into SNES Work RAM.

| Byte | Content |
|---|---|
| 0 | Command*8+Length (fixed length=1) |
| 1 | SNES Destination Address, low |
| 2 | SNES Destination Address, high |
| 3 | SNES Destination Address, bank number |
| 4 | Number of bytes to write ($01-$0B) |
| 5+ | Data Bytes (up to 11) |

Unused bytes at the end should be set to zero. Restricted to a single packet; not more than 11 bytes at once. Free Addresses in SNES memory: Bank 0 $1800-$1FFF, Bank $7F $0000-$FFFF.

## SGB Command $10 — DATA_TRN
Used to transfer binary code or data directly into SNES RAM via VRAM-Transfer (4 KBytes).

| Byte | Content |
|---|---|
| 0 | Command*8+Length (fixed length=1) |
| 1 | SNES Destination Address, low |
| 2 | SNES Destination Address, high |
| 3 | SNES Destination Address, bank number |
| 4-F | Not used (zero) |

The data is sent by VRAM-Transfer (4 KBytes). Free Addresses: Bank 0 $1800-$1FFF, Bank $7F $0000-$FFFF. Transfer length fixed at 4KBytes; directly writing to the free 2KBytes at 0:$1800 would be problematic.

## SGB Command $12 — JUMP
Used to set the SNES program counter and NMI (vblank interrupt) handler to specific addresses.

| Byte | Content |
|---|---|
| 0 | Command*8+Length (fixed length=1) |
| 1-3 | SNES Program Counter (low, high, bank) |
| 4-6 | SNES NMI Handler (low, high, bank) |
| 7-F | Not used, zero |

The game *Space Invaders* uses this function when selecting "Arcade mode" to execute SNES program code previously transferred from the SGB to the SNES. The SNES CPU is a Ricoh 5A22 (65C816 core + custom memory controller).

Notes for SNES programmers using flash cartridge in SGB as storage server:
- JUMP overwrites the NMI handler even if it is $000000.
- The SGB system software does not appear to use NMIs.
- JUMP can return to SGB system software via a 16-bit RTS. To do this, JML to a location in bank $00 containing byte value $60 (stubbed commands).
- IRQs and COP and BRK instructions are not useful because their handlers still point into SGB ROM. Use SEI WAI.
- If a program called through JUMP does not intend to return, it can overwrite all SNES RAM except $0000BB-$0000BD (the NMI vector).
- To enter APU boot ROM, write $FE to $2140. Echo will still be on though.

text:
# Color Attribute Commands

## SGB Command $04 — ATTR_BLK
Used to specify color attributes for the inside or outside of one or more rectangular screen regions.

| Byte | Content |
|---|---|
| 0 | Command*8+Length (length=1..7) |
| 1 | Number of Data Sets ($01..$12) |
| 2-7 | Data Set #1: Control Code (Bit 0=Change inside, Bit 1=Change surrounding line, Bit 2=Change outside), Color Palette Designation (Bits 0-1=inside palette, Bits 2-3=surrounding palette, Bits 4-5=outside palette), Coordinates X1(left) Y1(upper) X2(right) Y2(lower) |
| 8-D | Data Set #2 (if any) |
| E-F | Data Set #3 (continued at 0-3 in next packet if any) |

When changing only the Inside or Outside, the Surrounding line becomes automatically changed to same color. When sending three or more data sets, data is continued in further packets.

## SGB Command $05 — ATTR_LIN
Used to specify color attributes of one or more horizontal or vertical character lines.

| Byte | Content |
|---|---|
| 0 | Command*8+Length (length=1..7) |
| 1 | Number of Data Sets ($01..$6E), one byte each |
| 2+ | Each data set: Bits 0-4=Line Number (X or Y coordinate depending on bit 7), Bits 5-6=Palette Number (0-3), Bit 7=H/V Mode (0=Vertical, 1=Horizontal) |

When sending 15 or more data sets, data is continued in further packets. Lines from lastmost data sets overwrite lines from previous data sets if they overlap.

## SGB Command $06 — ATTR_DIV
Used to split the screen into two halves, and assign separate color attributes to each half and to the division line between them.

| Byte | Content |
|---|---|
| 0 | Command*8+Length (fixed length=1) |
| 1 | Bits 0-1=Palette below/right of division, Bits 2-3=Palette above/left, Bits 4-5=Division line palette, Bit 6=H/V Mode (0=split left/right, 1=split above/below) |
| 2 | X or Y Coordinate (depending on H/V bit) |

## SGB Command $07 — ATTR_CHR
Used to specify color attributes for separate characters.

| Byte | Content |
|---|---|
| 0 | Command*8+Length (length=1..6) |
| 1-2 | Beginning X and Y Coordinate |
| 3-4 | Number of Data Sets (1-360) |
| 5 | Writing Style: 0=Left to Right, 1=Top to Bottom |
| 6+ | Data Sets packed 4 per byte (Set 1 in MSBs, Set 4 in LSBs), each set is two bits indicating palette number for one character |

Data wraps to the next row/column when reaching the end of the screen. When sending 41 or more data sets, data continues in further packets.
